turns.content: Can you explain how WAR is calculated? And why do fWAR and bWAR give different numbers for the same player?

WAR (Wins Above Replacement) is an attempt to summarize a player’s total value in one number: how many more wins that player contributed than a “replacement-level” player (a readily available minor leaguer / bench fill-in) would have contributed in the same playing time. The broad structure is similar across versions: 1) Estimate runs the player contributed above some baseline in each component (batting, baserunning, fielding, pitching). 2) Add a positional adjustment (because shortstop value is harder to replace than first base, etc.). 3) Add a “replacement level” adjustment (because even a replacement player is not worth 0 runs; replacement is below average). 4) Convert runs to wins using a runs-per-win factor (roughly ~9–10 runs per win, varying by run environment). 5) Make league/park adjustments and scale to a season. Why fWAR and bWAR differ: they share the same concept but differ in (a) what underlying data they use for offense/defense/pitching, (b) how they treat defense, (c) how they convert pitcher performance into runs, and (d) some modeling choices (park factors, league factors, replacement levels, run-to-win conversion). Key differences: - Position players: Both use similar batting runs (based on linear weights), but defensive runs differ a lot. FanGraphs uses UZR (or now often OAA-based components in some contexts, but their published WAR is historically UZR-based), while Baseball-Reference uses DRS (Defensive Runs Saved) as the core fielding metric (plus some adjustments). Those can disagree materially, especially in single seasons. - Pitchers: FanGraphs pitcher WAR is FIP-based by default (fielding-independent: strikeouts, walks, HBP, HR), while Baseball-Reference pitcher WAR is RA9-based (runs allowed per 9 innings) with adjustments for defense and context. So a pitcher who outperforms his FIP (low BABIP, high strand rate) will often have higher bWAR than fWAR, and vice versa. If you want, I can walk through a simplified calculation for a hitter and a pitcher and show exactly where the two sites diverge.
Yes. Start with a hitter. Give me the components and the math in a simplified but still faithful way.

A simplified “position player WAR” framework looks like: WAR ≈ (Batting Runs + Baserunning Runs + Fielding Runs + Positional Adjustment + Replacement Runs) / RunsPerWin Where each “runs” term is relative to average unless noted. 1) Batting Runs This is typically derived from a linear-weights batting measure: - FanGraphs uses wRAA (weighted runs above average), based on wOBA. wRAA = ( (wOBA − league wOBA) / wOBA scale ) × PA This yields runs above average from batting. Baseball-Reference uses “Rbat” (batting runs) derived from linear weights on events (similar idea; slightly different coefficients and baselines). In practice, wRAA and Rbat are usually close, but not identical. 2) Baserunning Runs FanGraphs: BsR, which combines: - UBR (advancement on balls in play) - wSB (stolen base runs) - wGDP (avoiding double plays) Total is runs above average. Baseball-Reference: “Rbaser” (baserunning runs) includes SB/CS and other baserunning components. Again, similar intention, not identical inputs. 3) Fielding Runs FanGraphs: UZR (Ultimate Zone Rating) in runs above average, often with a positional “arm” component for OF, etc. Baseball-Reference: DRS (Defensive Runs Saved), plus some positional adjustments and sometimes extra components like catcher framing (in some implementations) or double-play ability for infielders. They also incorporate a “rfield” term. This is a major divergence point because UZR and DRS are different models of defense. 4) Positional Adjustment This accounts for defensive spectrum: shortstop/catcher/CF are harder, 1B/DH easier. It’s a run value per 1,350 defensive innings (roughly a full season): Common approximate values (varies slightly by source and year): - C: +12.5 - SS: +7.5 - 2B: +2.5 - 3B: +2.5 - CF: +2.5 - LF/RF: −7.5 - 1B: −12.5 - DH: −17.5 Then prorate by innings at each position. FG and BR use similar positional values but not always identical; small differences matter. 5) Replacement Runs This is the “replacement level” baseline: average is not replacement. Replacement is about ~20 runs below average per 600 PA for hitters (exact value depends on run environment). So you add a positive number to move from “above average” to “above replacement.” On FanGraphs, replacement runs are often computed as: Replacement Runs ≈ (Replacement level per PA) × PA where replacement level is set such that replacement team wins about .294 (historical convention). Numbers vary by season. 6) RunsPerWin Convert runs to wins, roughly: RunsPerWin ≈ 9 to 10 (higher in high-scoring eras) Both sources use a season-specific conversion. Example (simplified): Suppose a player has: - Batting: +25 runs - Baserunning: +3 runs - Fielding: +8 runs - Positional: +2 runs - Replacement: +20 runs Total runs above replacement = 25+3+8+2+20 = 58 runs If runs per win = 10, WAR ≈ 5.8 Differences between fWAR and bWAR for hitters mostly come from fielding (UZR vs DRS) and slightly from batting/baserunning inputs and positional/replacement constants.
Can you unpack the replacement level piece more? How do they decide what replacement is, and why is it about .294?

Replacement level is a convention meant to represent the talent level available for minimal cost: freely available call-ups, waiver claims, bench depth. It is not “0 value” in the sense of no runs; it’s a level of performance below league average. Why .294 winning percentage? Historically, analysts (notably in early WAR development) sought a baseline that approximates the performance of a team made up entirely of replacement players. A common target was around a .294 win% (about 47–48 wins in a 162-game season). This number is partly empirical (based on observed performance of readily available players) and partly chosen to make the framework consistent and stable across eras. How it translates to runs: If league average is 0 runs above average, replacement is negative runs relative to average. For hitters, replacement is often around −20 runs per 600 PA (give or take). For pitchers, replacement might be around +0.38 runs per 9 worse than average (again depends on run environment and the specific WAR implementation). In WAR arithmetic, most component stats are “above average.” To get to “above replacement,” you add “replacement runs,” which is effectively: Replacement Runs = (Average − Replacement) × playing time So a full-time player gets a bigger replacement credit because the alternative—filling those PA/innings with replacement talent—would be worse. The exact calibration can differ: - FanGraphs sets replacement level separately for hitters and pitchers and ties it to league run scoring and playing time distribution. - Baseball-Reference similarly sets replacement baselines but may use slightly different constants and conversions. A key subtlety: replacement level is not “the worst players in MLB.” It’s “freely available talent,” which can be better than the worst MLB regulars (because some bad regulars still have guaranteed contracts, service time considerations, etc.). Replacement is an economic concept as much as a talent concept.
For batting runs, you mentioned wRAA using wOBA. Can you show the formula with the scaling and explain what wOBA scale is doing?

Sure. wOBA (weighted On-Base Average) assigns linear weights to offensive events (BB, HBP, 1B, 2B, 3B, HR) to approximate their run value. It’s scaled to look like OBP (around .320–.340 typically), but it’s not a probability; it’s a weighted rate. wRAA (weighted runs above average) converts a player’s wOBA into runs above average: wRAA = ((wOBA − league_wOBA) / wOBA_scale) × PA Where: - wOBA is the player’s weighted on-base average. - league_wOBA is the league-average wOBA (often park-adjusted depending on the published stat). - PA is plate appearances. - wOBA_scale is a season-specific constant that converts points of wOBA into runs per PA. Why do you need wOBA_scale? Because wOBA is scaled to resemble OBP, not runs. A difference of 0.010 in wOBA does not directly equal 0.010 runs per PA. The scale translates “wOBA points above average” into “runs per PA above average.” Conceptually: - (wOBA − lg_wOBA) gives you how much better the player is in the wOBA rate scale. - Dividing by wOBA_scale converts that rate difference into runs per PA. - Multiplying by PA converts rate to total runs. Example with rough numbers: - Player wOBA = .370 - League wOBA = .320 - Difference = .050 - Suppose wOBA_scale = 1.25 (typical-ish) Runs per PA above avg = .050 / 1.25 = .040 If PA = 650, wRAA ≈ 26 runs. Different seasons have different run environments, so the scale changes accordingly.
How does park adjustment interact with wRAA? Is wOBA park-adjusted first or do they adjust runs later?

Implementations vary, but the typical approach is to park-adjust the offensive output to a neutral context, then compute runs above average (or adjust the resulting runs). FanGraphs generally provides wRC+ and wRAA in ways that reflect park and league context: - wRC+ is explicitly park- and league-adjusted (100 = league average). - wRAA on FanGraphs is typically park-adjusted as part of their wOBA-based run estimation framework (they have “park-adjusted wOBA” concepts in their calculations). There are two equivalent conceptual routes: A) Adjust the rate stat (wOBA or wRC) for park, then convert to runs. B) Convert to runs in the player’s context, then apply a park factor to translate to neutral runs. Because park factors are multiplicative or additive depending on modeling, route B can be tricky; most systems do something closer to A or use a hybrid. Baseball-Reference’s batting runs (Rbat) are also context-adjusted, but their park factors and the exact method differ (they use multi-year park factors and handle league context by season). In practice, park effects are usually modest for most players, but for extreme parks (Coors Field, Petco in earlier eras, etc.) they can matter a lot.
Let’s move to fielding. Why do UZR and DRS disagree, and what are the main modeling differences?

UZR and DRS both aim to estimate how many runs a fielder saved relative to an average fielder at the same position, but they differ in: 1) Data sources and batted-ball classification - UZR historically uses zones on the field and batted-ball types (ground ball, line drive, fly ball, pop-up) and estimates out probabilities by zone/type, then credits fielders for converting plays above/below expectation. - DRS (from Sports Info Solutions) uses a more granular video-scouting-based system (BIS data historically, SIS now) with detailed batted-ball locations, types, velocities (in later eras), and distinguishes more play types (e.g., “good plays,” “misplays,” outfield arm, double-play turns, bunts). 2) Components included - UZR includes range, errors, arm (OF), double-play (IF), and maybe some position-specific tweaks. - DRS includes range and positioning, plus separate components like outfield arm runs, catcher blocking, infield double-play ability, bunts, and also a system of “plus/minus” plays. 3) Treatment of positioning and shifts Modern defense involves heavy shifting. If a system attributes the result to the fielder who made the play without properly accounting for where he was positioned (and why), it can misattribute credit. Newer versions attempt to account for this, but historical series differ. 4) Sample size and noise Defense is noisy. Single-season estimates can vary widely. UZR itself is often recommended to be regressed or averaged over multiple seasons. DRS can also be volatile, but sometimes is considered slightly more stable in certain roles; opinions differ. 5) Run conversion and baselines Even if two systems agree on “outs above average,” converting to runs depends on the run value of each out type and situation. Systems may use different run expectancy tables. Because WAR uses fielding runs directly, a 10-run swing in defense is about 1 WAR (given ~10 runs per win). That’s why fWAR vs bWAR differences can be substantial for defense-first players.
Can you show a worked example where defense drives a big gap between fWAR and bWAR for a position player, with hypothetical numbers?

Sure. Suppose a shortstop plays 1,350 innings (full season) and has: Offense and baserunning (these are similar across sources): - Batting runs: +10 - Baserunning: +2 Positional adjustment (SS): +7.5 Replacement runs (full-time): +20 Now defense: - UZR says: +15 runs (excellent range year) - DRS says: +3 runs (good but not elite) Compute total runs above replacement: FanGraphs-like: Runs = 10 + 2 + 15 + 7.5 + 20 = 54.5 WAR ≈ 54.5 / 10 = 5.45 Baseball-Reference-like: Runs = 10 + 2 + 3 + 7.5 + 20 = 42.5 WAR ≈ 4.25 Difference: ~1.2 WAR, almost entirely driven by defense. If you also allow small differences in batting baselines and replacement constants, you might see 1.0–1.5 WAR gaps without anything “wrong”—just different defensive measurements.
Now pitching. Explain fWAR for pitchers in detail: FIP, runs, and replacement.

FanGraphs pitcher WAR is built around FIP (Fielding Independent Pitching), which estimates a pitcher’s run prevention based on outcomes largely controlled by the pitcher: strikeouts, walks, hit batters, and home runs. FIP formula (common form): FIP = (13*HR + 3*(BB+HBP) − 2*K) / IP + constant The constant sets league-average FIP equal to league-average ERA each season. How it becomes WAR: 1) Convert FIP to runs allowed (or runs above average) over innings pitched. A typical method: Pitching Runs Above Average (pRAA) = (lgFIP − pitcherFIP) * (IP / 9) * (runs-per-9 scaling) Often it’s effectively: pRAA = (lgFIP − FIP) * IP / 9 Because FIP is already on an “ERA-like” scale (runs per 9), you can treat the difference as runs per 9 (though FIP is based on earned runs scale; the WAR framework aligns it to runs). 2) Add replacement level Replacement for pitchers is set as a worse-than-average FIP baseline. FanGraphs uses a replacement level around 0.380 winning percentage for pitchers? More precisely, they define replacement runs per inning so that a replacement pitcher is some runs worse than average per 9, and then: Runs Above Replacement = Runs Above Average + Replacement Runs Replacement Runs = (lgFIP − replFIP) * IP / 9 (with sign conventions carefully handled) 3) Convert runs to wins WAR = Runs Above Replacement / RunsPerWin Why choose FIP? It stabilizes faster than ERA and tries to measure pitcher “true talent” in the things he most controls. It also avoids giving pitchers extra credit/penalty for team defense and sequencing (strand rate). But it means a contact manager who induces weak contact and consistently beats FIP may look worse in fWAR than in an ERA/RA9-based system.
Now bWAR pitching. How does the RA9 approach work and how do they handle defense?

Baseball-Reference pitcher WAR starts from runs allowed per 9 innings (RA9), which is based on actual runs allowed (including unearned runs in some versions; they have RA9 and sometimes ERA-based variants). The basic idea: 1) Measure pitcher’s actual run prevention: RA9 = (Runs Allowed / IP) * 9 2) Compare to league average, adjust for park: RA9_adj accounts for park and league scoring. 3) Credit/penalize based on runs allowed relative to average, scaled by innings: Runs Above Average = (lgRA9_adj − pitcherRA9_adj) * IP / 9 4) Adjust for team defense and catcher effects (in some formulations) They attempt to isolate the pitcher’s contribution by accounting for the defensive quality behind him. This is conceptually hard: if a pitcher’s team converts more balls in play into outs, his RA9 looks better, but perhaps the defense deserves some credit. Baseball-Reference uses a defensive adjustment based on team fielding (and perhaps component defensive metrics) to apportion some of that. 5) Add replacement runs and convert to wins. So bWAR tends to reward pitchers for “actual runs prevented,” including sequencing, contact quality, and perhaps luck, but then tries to correct for defense and park. fWAR rewards pitchers for underlying peripherals, more predictive and less dependent on context.
People argue about “what WAR should be.” Is WAR supposed to be descriptive (what happened) or predictive (true talent)?

Different WAR flavors implicitly answer different questions: - Descriptive WAR: “How much value did the player actually provide in the games that were played?” RA9-based pitcher WAR leans descriptive because it uses actual runs allowed. Position-player WAR is mostly descriptive too, but defense metrics introduce modeling. - Predictive WAR: “How good is the player, independent of luck/defense/sequence?” FIP-based pitcher WAR leans predictive because it strips out much of the noise and context. Even within a single framework, WAR is a hybrid: - Batting linear weights are descriptive but quite stable. - Defense metrics are descriptive but noisy and model-dependent. - Pitching can be either descriptive (RA9) or more talent-based (FIP). So when someone asks “Who was more valuable last year?” a descriptive measure is appealing. When someone asks “Who will be better next year?” FIP-based measures or projection systems are better. WAR is often used for both, but it’s important to match the metric to the question.
Let’s dig into runs-to-wins conversion. Why is it ~10 runs per win and how do they compute it for a season?

The relationship between runs and wins comes from the Pythagorean expectation / run distribution. Roughly, in MLB scoring environments, an extra ~10 runs corresponds to about one additional win over a full season’s worth of games, but it varies. Higher scoring environment: - Each run is “less valuable” in terms of changing win probability because games have more runs; marginal runs translate into slightly fewer wins. So runs per win increases (maybe 10.2–10.6). Lower scoring environment: - Each run is “more valuable” because runs are scarcer; runs per win decreases (maybe 9.2–9.7). How computed: A common method is to estimate: RunsPerWin ≈ ( (lgR + lgRA) / (2 * lgG) ) * constant But more practically, WAR providers compute a season-specific “runs to wins” factor from empirical relationships between run differential and wins in that season, or use a formula derived from Pythagorean expectation with an exponent. FanGraphs publishes “R/Win” each season. Baseball-Reference similarly has a conversion embedded in their WAR. So the conversion is not fixed at 10; it’s calibrated to the season’s run environment.
I want to understand the exact connection between linear weights batting runs and team runs. Are batting runs “context neutral”? How do they treat RBI-type sequencing?

Linear weights (wOBA/wRAA, or similar) are context-neutral: they value events by their average run impact across all base/out states, not by the specific inning/game context. Example: A home run is worth about ~1.4 runs in linear weights, regardless of whether it was a solo shot or a grand slam in actual scoring. That’s because on average, a home run occurs with some distribution of men on base; linear weights bake in that average. This avoids crediting hitters for “clutch” sequencing that may be largely random year-to-year. It also means batting runs won’t exactly match actual runs created in a season for a player, because actual context (teammates on base, etc.) can differ from average. Some systems also provide context-dependent measures: - RE24: runs added based on actual base/out state - WPA: win probability added based on leverage and game state But WAR generally uses context-neutral batting because it aims to measure underlying offensive contribution independent of lineup context.
How do they handle double counting between hitting and baserunning? For example, a single includes taking extra bases sometimes.

Good question. The separation is typically: - Batting runs: value of the batting event itself to reach base and advance runners in an average way (linear weights assume average advancement on hits, etc.). - Baserunning runs: value from deviations from average advancement—taking extra bases on singles/doubles, tagging up, first-to-third, scoring from first, etc.—and stolen base value and avoiding double plays. To avoid double counting, baserunning metrics like UBR are built by comparing actual advancement to expected advancement given the event and context, then crediting the runner for the difference. So the hit’s “base value” is in batting; the runner’s extra base taken (or not taken) is in baserunning. Similarly, wGDP isolates the cost/benefit of grounding into double plays relative to average in those situations, rather than mixing it into batting event weights.
Does WAR treat catchers differently? Framing, blocking, game-calling—how is that integrated?

Catchers are a special case because they contribute in ways not captured by traditional fielding metrics. - FanGraphs: Historically, catcher defense in fWAR was based on fielding runs that include blocking and throwing, and later framing was added (for certain time ranges) as a separate “Framing” component, depending on the dataset and year. Their current catcher defense includes pitch framing runs where available. - Baseball-Reference: bWAR includes catcher defense components too (rCERA historically existed but was phased out; more recent catcher defense uses DRS-based components, which include blocking and throwing, and framing in later years when data supports it). However, the inclusion varies by era: - Before PITCHf/x/Statcast-type tracking, framing estimates are not available, so catcher WAR may be missing an important component. - Even with data, framing models differ and can cause big differences in catcher WAR across sites. So catcher WAR is often less comparable across long historical spans and can differ between fWAR and bWAR depending on framing model and whether it’s included.
Let’s talk about positional adjustment more rigorously. Why are the values what they are? Is it based on offense distribution by position?

Positional adjustments are intended to reflect the defensive difficulty and scarcity of competent defenders at each position. There are two main conceptual justifications: 1) Empirical offensive differences by position Historically, positions like SS/C/CF have lower offensive output because teams prioritize defense there. Positions like 1B/LF/RF/DH tend to have higher offense. If you compare average hitting by position, you see systematic gaps. 2) Defensive spectrum and replacement scarcity If it’s hard to find a competent shortstop, then an average-hitting shortstop is more valuable than an average-hitting first baseman, because the baseline for “acceptable shortstop defense” is higher. How the actual run values are set: Analysts estimate how much defensive value (in runs) is required to make players at different positions comparable, often by looking at: - Players who switch positions (e.g., SS to 3B, CF to LF) and how their offensive/defensive profiles change. - The distribution of talent and observed run prevention differences by position. - Regression modeling to infer the defensive difficulty. Then they set constants so that, roughly, an average player at each position has similar overall value when including typical offense/defense at that position. Because it’s a constant, it’s a blunt instrument, but it’s necessary to avoid systematically overvaluing bat-only positions. Small differences in the constants can affect WAR by a few tenths for multi-position players.
How does WAR handle players who split time across positions, including DH?

The positional adjustment is prorated by innings (or games) at each position. Example: A player logs: - 700 innings at 2B (pos adj ~ +2.5 per 1350) - 500 innings at SS (pos adj ~ +7.5 per 1350) - 150 innings at DH (pos adj ~ −17.5 per 1350) Compute: PosRuns = (700/1350)*2.5 + (500/1350)*7.5 + (150/1350)*(-17.5) Then add that positional runs to the rest of the components. This is why a part-time SS who mostly plays 2B still gets some SS credit, and a player who DHs a lot gets penalized. DH is penalized because it has near-zero defensive responsibility and is easiest to fill with a bat.
What about “league adjustments”? How do they compare AL and NL, or different eras?

League adjustments address differences in league quality and run environment. In modern MLB, AL vs NL differences are generally small, but historically they have mattered. - Run environment: If one league scores more runs, raw stats need context. Park factors and league factors handle this. - DH rule: The presence of the DH changes run scoring and the baseline for pitchers/hitters. WAR calculations incorporate league context so that a hitter in the AL and a hitter in the NL are compared fairly, and pitchers are compared with awareness of league scoring. - Era: WAR is designed to be cross-era comparable by using league-average baselines and run-to-win conversion by season. That said, data quality differences (especially defense) make cross-era comparisons less precise. In practice: - Batting runs are computed relative to league average for that season/league. - Park factors adjust to neutral. - Replacement levels and R/Win are season-specific. So a 5 WAR season in 1968 and a 5 WAR season in 1999 are meant to represent similar “wins added,” though the uncertainty differs.
I’ve heard criticism that WAR is not additive across players because of interaction effects. Is WAR additive? Can you sum team WAR to estimate team wins?

WAR is approximately additive, and people do sum it to approximate team wins, but with caveats. In theory: Team Wins ≈ Replacement Team Wins + Team WAR If replacement team is ~48 wins, and your roster totals 40 WAR, you’d expect ~88 wins. In practice, deviations occur because: - Playing time allocation: WAR assumes a replacement player fills the “missing” playing time. If your bench is better/worse than replacement, team totals shift. - Bullpen leverage: WAR for relievers is context-neutral; actual win impact depends on leverage. - Defense interactions: Two fielders’ ranges can overlap; shifting can reassign responsibilities. Metrics attempt to allocate credit, but additivity is imperfect. - Sequencing and clustering: Context-neutral offense doesn’t capture sequencing; a team with the same linear weights distribution can score slightly different actual runs due to clustering of events. Still, team WAR correlates strongly with wins, and it’s a useful approximation.
Let’s do a deeper numerical example for a hitter using wOBA to wRAA to WAR, including park factor, positional adjustment, and R/Win. Use plausible constants.

Let’s construct a plausible example. Assume season constants: - League wOBA = .320 - wOBA scale = 1.25 - R/Win = 9.8 - Replacement runs per PA: 0.030 (this yields ~18 runs per 600 PA; plausible order of magnitude) - Player park factor: 105 (5% hitter-friendly). We’ll apply a park adjustment to neutralize: divide offensive runs by 1.05 (simplified). Player line: - PA = 650 - Player wOBA = .360 - BsR = +4 - Fielding (UZR) = +6 - Innings: 1200 at CF, 150 at LF Positional constants (per 1350 inn): - CF +2.5 - LF −7.5 Step 1: wRAA (unadjusted) wRAA_raw = ((.360 − .320) / 1.25) * 650 Difference = .040 .040 / 1.25 = .032 runs/PA above avg *650 = 20.8 runs above avg Step 2: Park-adjust batting runs (simplified) Neutral batting runs ≈ 20.8 / 1.05 = 19.8 Step 3: Add baserunning and fielding Runs so far above avg = 19.8 + 4 + 6 = 29.8 Step 4: Positional adjustment PosRuns = (1200/1350)*2.5 + (150/1350)*(-7.5) = 0.8889*2.5 + 0.1111*(-7.5) = 2.22 − 0.83 = +1.39 runs Runs above avg including position = 29.8 + 1.39 = 31.19 Step 5: Replacement runs ReplRuns = 0.030 * 650 = 19.5 Runs above replacement = 31.19 + 19.5 = 50.69 Step 6: Convert to WAR WAR = 50.69 / 9.8 = 5.17 WAR This is the skeleton of a FanGraphs-style position player WAR. A Baseball-Reference-style calculation would have similar structure but might differ in: - Batting run derivation and park factor application - Fielding metric (DRS) - Positional constants - Replacement runs constant - R/Win
For pitchers, can you do a similar numerical example showing how the same pitcher could have higher bWAR than fWAR because he “beats FIP”?

Yes. Suppose a starter throws 180 IP. League context: - lgFIP = 4.20 - lgRA9 = 4.50 (runs allowed per 9; includes unearned, etc.) - R/Win = 9.8 Replacement baseline: - replFIP = 5.20 (1.00 worse than league; illustrative) - replRA9 = 5.50 (also 1.00 worse; illustrative) Pitcher results: - FIP = 4.10 (slightly better than league) - RA9 = 3.60 (much better than league; due to low BABIP/high LOB%/weak contact) FanGraphs-style (FIP-based): Runs above average (FIP) = (lgFIP − FIP) * IP / 9 = (4.20 − 4.10) * 180/9 = 0.10 * 20 = 2.0 runs above avg Replacement runs (FIP) = (replFIP − lgFIP) * IP / 9 Be careful with sign: replacement is worse, so you add credit: = (5.20 − 4.20) * 20 = 1.00 * 20 = 20 runs Runs above replacement = 2 + 20 = 22 runs WAR = 22 / 9.8 = 2.24 fWAR Baseball-Reference-style (RA9-based): Runs above average = (lgRA9 − RA9) * IP / 9 = (4.50 − 3.60) * 20 = 0.90 * 20 = 18 runs above avg Replacement runs = (replRA9 − lgRA9) * IP / 9 = (5.50 − 4.50) * 20 = 20 runs Runs above replacement = 18 + 20 = 38 runs WAR = 38 / 9.8 = 3.88 bWAR Difference: ~1.6 WAR, driven by RA9 being much better than FIP suggests. In real calculations, bWAR also adjusts for quality of defense behind the pitcher and park; that might reduce or increase the gap.
What about relievers? How does leverage come into pitcher WAR, if at all?

In most standard WAR implementations, leverage is not directly included. WAR is context-neutral: it values runs prevented equally regardless of inning/score. That means a shutdown closer and a middle reliever with identical RA9/FIP and innings get similar WAR, even though the closer’s outs occurred in higher leverage. FanGraphs does publish “WPA” and “WPA/LI” and “Clutch,” which incorporate leverage and context, but those are separate from WAR. Baseball-Reference similarly provides WPA and leverage stats separately. There have been proposals for “leveraged WAR” or adding a leverage multiplier for relievers, but standard WAR sticks to context-neutral valuation to avoid mixing role usage with intrinsic performance. Some indirect effects exist: - Relievers have smaller innings totals, so their WAR totals are usually smaller. - Replacement level for relievers and starters can differ because of role and usage patterns. - FanGraphs also has a “RA9-WAR” for pitchers as an alternative to FIP-WAR, which can be used for relievers if you want descriptive run prevention.
I keep hearing that “WAR is not a stat, it’s a framework.” What does that mean in practice?

It means WAR is a composite metric built from multiple sub-metrics and assumptions. Unlike, say, OBP (which is directly computed from events), WAR requires: - Choosing a run estimator for offense (wOBA, linear weights, etc.) - Choosing a defensive metric (UZR, DRS, OAA, etc.) - Choosing a pitching model (FIP vs RA9, defense adjustments) - Setting replacement levels - Setting positional adjustments - Applying park and league adjustments - Converting runs to wins Different choices yield different WAR implementations. The “WAR framework” is the idea: total value in wins relative to replacement. The exact number depends on the ingredients. So when comparing fWAR and bWAR, you’re seeing two instantiations of the same framework with different ingredients.
Is there a way to reconcile them? Like an “average WAR” or a best practice for evaluation?

Common best practices: 1) Use both and understand why they differ If fWAR and bWAR agree, you can be more confident. If they differ, inspect the components: - For hitters: check fielding components and positional splits. - For pitchers: compare FIP vs RA9, defense/park contexts. 2) Use a multi-year average for defense Single-year defensive metrics are noisy. If you’re evaluating talent, a 3-year defensive average (regressed) is better. 3) Use role-appropriate pitcher metrics For projection: FIP, xFIP, SIERA, and projections (Steamer, ZiPS) are better. For awards/“what happened”: RA9-based can be informative, but still noisy. 4) If you must pick one - FanGraphs WAR is often favored for pitchers in predictive contexts. - Baseball-Reference WAR is often favored for descriptive pitcher value (actual runs allowed). For position players, differences are mostly defense, so neither is universally “right.” Some analysts average fWAR and bWAR as a rough consensus, but that’s not a principled solution—just a pragmatic way to reduce model-specific noise.
Let’s go even deeper on pitcher FIP-WAR. Why is the FIP constant needed, and does it cause issues when comparing across eras?

The FIP constant aligns FIP to the ERA scale so that league-average FIP equals league-average ERA in that season. Without it, FIP would be on an arbitrary scale because the linear weights (13, 3, -2) produce a run estimate but not necessarily the same baseline as ERA. FIP = (13*HR + 3*(BB+HBP) − 2*K) / IP + C Where C is chosen so: mean(FIP) = mean(ERA) for that league/season. Does it cause issues across eras? It helps comparability because it keeps FIP centered on the season’s run environment. But FIP itself assumes stable relationships between the components and run scoring. Across eras, the run value of HR, BB, K can change slightly due to ball/parks/strategy. Modern implementations update coefficients or use wOBA-like linear weights for events; classic FIP uses fixed coefficients that are a good approximation in modern baseball but not perfect historically. FanGraphs generally uses the standard coefficients but because the constant is season-specific, the scale stays aligned. Still, if you go far back, differences in scoring patterns (e.g., dead-ball era) might mean FIP is less faithful than in modern contexts.
What about xFIP and SIERA? Are there WAR versions based on those?

xFIP and SIERA are alternative estimators of pitcher performance: - xFIP replaces actual HR with expected HR based on fly balls and a league-average HR/FB rate. It treats HR as partly luck and regresses it. - SIERA models how strikeouts, walks, and batted-ball types interact, aiming to better predict ERA. WAR: Standard published WAR usually uses FIP (FanGraphs) or RA9 (Baseball-Reference). FanGraphs doesn’t generally publish “xFIP-WAR” or “SIERA-WAR” as the default headline, though you can conceptually build WAR from any run estimator by: Runs Above Average = (lgMetric − playerMetric) * IP / 9 + replacement then / R/Win. But because xFIP/SIERA are more “talent” estimators, WAR based on them would be more predictive than descriptive. It could be useful for forecasting or valuation but would be less aligned with “what happened.”
Let’s talk about uncertainty. Can we put error bars on WAR?

You can, and some analysts have tried, but official WAR numbers usually don’t display error bars. The uncertainty differs by component: - Batting runs: relatively low error; large sample of events, stable linear weights. Over a full season, maybe a few runs of uncertainty. - Baserunning: moderate; fewer events, but still somewhat stable. - Defense: high; measurement noise, scorer/model differences, positioning. Single-season defensive runs might have an uncertainty on the order of ±5 to ±10 runs or more depending on position and innings. - Pitching: depends on model. FIP stabilizes faster than ERA, but still has variance. RA9 is noisy due to sequencing and defense. Putting it together: A rough heuristic sometimes cited: for a full-season position player, WAR uncertainty might be around ±1 WAR; for partial seasons, larger. For pitchers, it can be similar or larger, especially for relievers due to small innings. These are not official, but they reflect that WAR should not be treated as exact to the decimal. Differences of 0.2 WAR are basically noise; even 1 WAR can be within uncertainty depending on components.
If defense is so noisy, why include it at all? Wouldn’t offense-only WAR be more reliable?

Excluding defense would systematically misvalue players. Defense is real value: preventing runs matters as much as creating them. The problem is measurement, not importance. Two points: 1) Bias vs variance tradeoff If you omit defense, you reduce variance (less noise) but introduce bias (you treat all defenders as average). That penalizes great defenders and overvalues poor ones. 2) Use multi-year/regressed defense A better approach is to include defense but shrink it toward average based on sample size (regression). Many projection systems do this. Single-season WAR is a descriptive measure and will be noisy, but over multiple seasons, the signal improves. So defense belongs in a “total value” framework; the key is to interpret it with appropriate skepticism and context.
How do they handle pitchers hitting in pre-DH or NL? Does pitcher batting affect pitcher WAR or team WAR?

Pitcher batting is treated as part of position-player offense, not pitcher WAR. WAR frameworks separate batting and pitching contributions: - A pitcher’s value as a pitcher is in pitching WAR. - If a pitcher also bats (pre-DH), his batting runs could be included in position-player WAR if you treat him as a hitter. Some databases may have “batting WAR” for pitchers as position players. In team totals, both contributions matter: - A pre-DH team got negative hitting from pitchers; that’s part of the team’s overall offensive output. - When comparing leagues, DH changes the baseline: the NL had pitcher PA that are mostly automatic outs, lowering run scoring. WAR’s league context adjustments handle this so players aren’t unfairly compared. For individual pitcher WAR, the batting is usually excluded; it’s a separate ledger.
What about two-way players? How do you combine WAR for someone who hits and pitches?

For a true two-way player, you can sum their pitching WAR and position-player WAR, because they are contributing in two separate roles with separate playing time. The main caution is: - Ensure the replacement level assumptions are consistent and you’re not double-counting roster spot value. But generally, if a player provides 3 WAR as a hitter and 3 WAR as a pitcher, total value is around 6 WAR. Some systems also consider the roster spot flexibility (one player doing two jobs), but standard WAR doesn’t explicitly add extra value for that; it’s already reflected in playing time and performance.
Let’s compare wRC+ and OPS+. Why do people prefer wRC+? And how does that feed into WAR?

OPS+ and wRC+ are both rate stats indexed to league average (100 = average), adjusted for park and league. OPS+: - Based on OPS = OBP + SLG. - OPS weights OBP and SLG equally, which is not optimal for run estimation (OBP is more valuable than SLG point-for-point). - OPS+ uses a formula to adjust for park and league, but it’s still based on OPS. wRC+: - Based on wOBA, which uses linear weights tuned to actual run values. - It’s scaled so 100 = league average, and each point is roughly a percent above/below league average in runs created. - More directly tied to run scoring, so it’s better for comparing hitters. Connection to WAR: - FanGraphs uses wRAA (from wOBA) as the batting runs component. - wRC+ is essentially the rate version of that (with scaling and adjustments). If you know wRC+ and PA, you can back into something close to batting runs above average, though you’d still need league runs per PA and scaling constants. So wRC+ is preferred because it’s a more accurate context-neutral run estimator.
Are there any systematic biases in WAR regarding certain player types? Like extreme groundball pitchers, or fast contact hitters?

Potential biases exist because WAR depends on models: Pitchers: - fWAR (FIP-based) may undervalue pitchers who consistently suppress BABIP or induce weak contact if that skill is real and persistent. Research suggests some pitchers have some influence on contact quality, but historically FIP assumed limited control over balls in play. - bWAR (RA9-based) may overvalue pitchers who had good luck on balls in play or sequencing in a single season. Hitters: - Linear weights are pretty robust, but certain skills like hitting into shifts, speed effects on infield hits, and interaction with defense positioning may be imperfectly captured. - Baserunning metrics may miss subtle value like forcing defensive errors or altering pitcher behavior, though they capture a lot. Fielding: - Defensive metrics can be biased by positioning, scorer differences, and the interaction of staff tendencies (pitcher induces balls to certain zones) with fielder opportunities. Statcast-era metrics try to address this with more granular tracking. So yes: WAR is the best widely used summary metric, but it inherits the limitations of its components.
You mentioned Statcast-era metrics like OAA. Why don’t WAR systems just use OAA for defense?

Some do incorporate Statcast-based defense in some contexts, but there are constraints: - Coverage: Statcast is available from mid-2010s onward. For historical continuity, WAR providers maintain series that go back decades, which favors UZR/DRS-like approaches. - Metric differences: OAA is a probability-based out model based on tracking data, but converting it to runs requires modeling (e.g., run value of prevented hits by type, throwing, etc.). It’s doable, but needs careful calibration. - Components: OAA for infield/outfield range is strong, but full defensive value includes arm, double plays, catcher skills, etc. Statcast has components for arm and framing now, but integrating them consistently is complex. - Stability and back-testing: WAR providers are cautious about changing core components because it breaks comparability and requires extensive validation. Over time, you’ll likely see more Statcast integration, but it’s gradual.
Let’s talk about “replacement player.” Is replacement level the same for all positions? It seems like it should vary.

Replacement level is generally set by playing time (PA/IP) and role, and positional adjustments handle much of the positional scarcity. In many WAR frameworks: - Replacement level for hitters is roughly uniform in terms of runs per PA below average, regardless of position. - Then positional adjustment accounts for how hard it is to play the position defensively. You could instead define position-specific replacement levels (a replacement shortstop might be worse offensively than a replacement first baseman), but that risks double counting scarcity if you also apply positional adjustments. Most systems keep replacement level fairly uniform and let positional adjustment do the work. That said, some subtle differences exist: - Catchers and shortstops might have different replacement assumptions in certain implementations because the pool of “freely available competent defenders” is smaller. - Pitcher replacement differs for starters vs relievers. But the standard approach is: replacement is a general baseline; position difficulty is handled separately.
How do they compute team defense adjustments for pitcher bWAR? I want more detail.

At a high level, Baseball-Reference attempts to separate pitcher performance from team defense by estimating how many runs the defense saved/cost relative to average, then apportioning some of that to pitchers. Conceptually: 1) A team’s runs allowed is a function of: - Pitching (strikeouts, walks, HR, contact management, sequencing) - Defense (converting balls in play into outs, limiting extra bases) - Park and scoring environment 2) If a team defense is +30 runs by a defensive metric, the pitchers’ RA9 will look better than if the defense were average. To avoid giving pitchers full credit for that, bWAR applies a “defense adjustment” to pitcher runs allowed, effectively raising their “credit” RA9 when defense was good (and lowering it when defense was bad). Implementation details can involve: - Using team DRS or similar fielding runs - Converting that to a per-inning or per-ball-in-play adjustment - Allocating defense credit across pitchers based on innings (and sometimes BIP faced) - Park adjustments layered in Because the exact allocation is complex and depends on data availability and the defensive metric used, it’s not perfect. But it’s an attempt to make RA9-based WAR fairer across contexts.
Does that mean bWAR can “double count” defense? Like, defense is in position-player WAR and also affects pitcher WAR adjustments.

It’s designed to avoid double counting, but the accounting is tricky. - Position-player WAR includes individual defensive runs for fielders. - Pitcher bWAR starts from team runs allowed and then adjusts to remove team defense effects so pitchers aren’t credited for fielders’ work. If done perfectly, the total team WAR from pitchers + fielders would align with team run prevention in a consistent way. In practice, because: - Defensive metrics aren’t perfect, - Allocation of team defense to pitchers is approximate, - Some pitcher skills might influence defensive metrics (pitchers who induce easy grounders), there can be some mismatch. FanGraphs avoids this particular issue in their default pitcher WAR by using FIP, which largely sidesteps team defense. But then you’re not measuring actual run prevention. So both approaches have tradeoffs.
Are there versions of WAR that use actual runs for pitchers but don’t do defense adjustments? What would that look like?

Yes—conceptually that’s a pure RA9-based WAR without defense adjustment. It would be: Runs Above Average = (lgRA9 − pitcherRA9) * IP/9 + replacement then / R/Win This would be fully descriptive of the pitcher’s actual run prevention, but it would credit pitchers for team defense and park effects unless you adjust for those. Park adjustment is relatively straightforward; defense is harder. FanGraphs publishes RA9-WAR as an alternative pitcher WAR, which is closer to this idea (though they may still adjust for park and league). It’s useful if you want “what happened” from runs allowed rather than peripherals.
Let’s switch to batting again. How does WAR treat intentional walks? Are they counted the same as BB in wOBA?

Different implementations treat IBB differently: - In wOBA, intentional walks are often excluded from the numerator and denominator (or weighted differently) because an IBB is more of a managerial decision than a hitter skill, and its run value differs. FanGraphs’ wOBA formula typically excludes IBB (using BB - IBB) and includes HBP, etc. - In linear weights, you can include IBB with a lower weight or exclude it; many modern batting run estimators exclude IBB to focus on hitter contribution. This choice affects batting runs slightly for hitters with many IBB (e.g., peak Bonds). For most players, it’s small. Baseball-Reference’s batting runs may treat IBB differently depending on era and their linear weights setup.
How does WAR handle stolen base attempts? Is it just SB*value - CS*value?

The core stolen base component is often: wSB = (SB * run_value_SB) + (CS * run_value_CS) − (expected value at league average attempt rate) More simply, you can approximate: Stolen base runs ≈ SB * 0.2 + CS * (−0.4) Values vary by era because the run value of an out or extra base changes with scoring environment. FanGraphs’ baserunning includes wSB as a component and then adds other baserunning (UBR) and GDP avoidance. wSB itself is computed relative to league average so that average baserunners are near zero. So it’s not just SB minus CS; it’s SB/CS valued in runs and compared to an average baseline.
You’ve mentioned “linear weights” a lot. How are linear weights derived?

Linear weights come from run expectancy tables: 1) Compute run expectancy for each base/out state (24 states: 0/1/2 outs × bases empty/1st/2nd/3rd combinations). Run expectancy = average runs scored from that state to end of inning. 2) For each event (single, double, BB, etc.), compute the average change in run expectancy plus runs scored on the play, across all instances of that event: Linear weight(event) ≈ average( RE(after) − RE(before) + runs_scored_on_play ) 3) That yields an average run value for each event. Because it’s averaged across contexts, it becomes context-neutral. Different datasets, eras, and definitions (e.g., excluding IBB) yield slightly different weights. wOBA weights are essentially normalized linear weights scaled to the OBP-like scale.
If linear weights are context-neutral, why do we need wRC+ instead of just wOBA?

wOBA is a rate stat on an OBP-like scale. It’s useful, but: - It’s not indexed (so it’s harder to compare across seasons without knowing league average). - It’s not directly in runs (though it can be converted via wRAA). - Park and league adjustments aren’t automatically obvious in raw wOBA. wRC+ solves that by: - Converting wOBA to runs created per PA - Adjusting for park and league - Indexing to 100 = league average So wRC+ is a more interpretable “how much better than average” metric, while wOBA is a building block.
Let’s talk about “replacement runs per PA” you used earlier. Where does 0.030 come from? Is it tied to .294?

Yes. Roughly, if a replacement team is expected to win about 48 games, that corresponds to a run differential consistent with that win% in that season’s run environment. One way to conceptualize hitter replacement: - League average hitter is 0 runs above average by definition. - Replacement hitter is set to be some runs below average per PA, often around −0.03 runs/PA (which is −18 runs per 600 PA). That means a full-time player gets +18 runs of “replacement credit” because replacing him with a replacement hitter would cost that many runs. The exact value changes with: - R/Win - The target replacement win% - The distribution of playing time and roster construction assumptions FanGraphs publishes replacement level and uses league totals to calibrate. Baseball-Reference similarly calibrates but may land on slightly different per-PA values. So 0.030 was a plausible illustrative number; the real number might be closer to 0.029–0.033 depending on season.
How do they prevent WAR from being inflated by playing time? Like, a mediocre player with lots of PA gets more WAR than a great part-timer.

WAR is designed to reflect total value, not rate value. Playing time is part of value: being good and playing a lot is more valuable than being good and playing little. To compare rate performance, you use: - wRC+ for hitters (rate) - FIP- or ERA- for pitchers (rate) - WAR per 600 PA or per 200 IP (rate) WAR includes replacement runs proportional to playing time, which means even an average-ish regular can accumulate WAR because the alternative (replacement) is worse. That’s intentional: a 2 WAR player might be only slightly above average per PA but provides a lot of value by being a competent everyday player. If you want to isolate “quality,” use rate stats or peak WAR, not just total WAR.
Can you explain why replacement is below average but still positive credit in WAR? That confuses people.

Most component stats are measured relative to average. Example: batting runs above average (wRAA) is 0 for an average hitter. But WAR is “above replacement,” not “above average.” Replacement is below average. So to convert above-average runs to above-replacement runs, you add a constant amount of runs proportional to playing time. Example: - Average hitter: 0 batting runs above average. If replacement level is −18 runs per 600 PA, then an average hitter over 600 PA is +18 runs above replacement from offense alone (plus position/fielding/baserunning, etc.). That’s why an average full-time position player is typically around 2 WAR (depending on position), not 0 WAR. So “positive replacement credit” is not a bonus; it’s the arithmetic needed to change baselines.
How do they set the average WAR of the league? Like, does the average player have 2 WAR?

The average “full-time” player is around 2 WAR, but the average “player-season” across everyone who appears (including bench players) is lower. Because: - Replacement level is below average, the average team has a certain amount of WAR above replacement built in. - Total WAR in the league is constrained by the replacement baseline and number of games. Roughly, total WAR across all players in MLB in a season is about (total wins above replacement teams). If replacement teams would win 48 each, then each real team wins (actual wins - 48) above replacement. Summed across 30 teams, total WAR is around total actual wins - 30*48. Since total actual wins is 2,430 (162*30 /2), total WAR is about 2,430 - 1,440 = 990 WAR. That’s a rough check; actual totals differ slightly due to rounding and modeling. So the framework is calibrated so that league totals make sense.
That league total check is interesting. Can you walk through it more carefully?

MLB has 30 teams, 162 games each, but each game produces one win and one loss, so total wins in the league = total games played = 30*162/2 = 2,430 wins. If replacement level is defined such that a replacement team would win about 48 games (0.294 * 162 ≈ 47.6), then: Replacement wins per team ≈ 48 Replacement wins for 30 teams ≈ 1,440 Then total wins above replacement in the league ≈ 2,430 − 1,440 = 990 WAR is intended to sum to wins above replacement, so you expect total WAR across all players ≈ 990, subject to: - Differences in replacement level calibration for hitters vs pitchers - How they handle pitchers hitting (historically) - Rounding and accounting differences - Any small mismatch due to park/league adjustments and run-to-win conversions This “sum-to-league-wins” property is one reason replacement level is a useful anchor.
Nice. Now, can you explain why WAR is sometimes negative? Who gets negative WAR?

A player can be negative WAR if their performance is worse than replacement level given their playing time. Example: - A hitter with very poor offense and/or defense who still gets a lot of PA can be below replacement. - A pitcher with very poor run prevention (or FIP) over significant innings can be below replacement. Negative WAR means the team would likely have been better off using a replacement-level player instead. It doesn’t mean the player is “bad” in an absolute sense—he’s still an MLB player—but relative to a freely available alternative, he cost wins. Small negative values can also come from noise, especially in defense or small samples.
How do small-sample call-ups get treated? Does WAR overreact?

In small samples, WAR can swing quickly because: - Batting events are discrete; a few HR or a few outs can change wOBA a lot. - Defensive metrics in small innings are extremely noisy. - Pitcher WAR can be volatile in a few innings, especially RA9-based. WAR providers typically publish the raw calculation without heavy regression, because it’s “what happened.” So yes, it can look extreme in small samples. For evaluation, you usually: - Use larger samples - Use projections - Regress defense and even offense toward average based on PA/IP So WAR is best interpreted as descriptive accounting, not a guarantee of true talent, especially in small samples.
What is “RAR” vs WAR? I see Runs Above Replacement.

RAR (Runs Above Replacement) is essentially the numerator of WAR before converting runs to wins. RAR = Batting + Baserunning + Fielding + Positional + Replacement (for hitters) or RAR = Pitching runs above average + replacement (for pitchers) Then: WAR = RAR / R/Win RAR is useful because: - It keeps everything in the same unit (runs) and can be combined with other run-based analysis. - It avoids the slight variability of R/Win across seasons if you want to compare within a season. But WAR is more interpretable for many people because wins are intuitive.
What about “WAA” (Wins Above Average)? How does that relate?

WAA is Wins Above Average, not above replacement. It’s essentially: WAA = (Runs Above Average) / R/Win Where “Runs Above Average” excludes the replacement runs component and usually excludes positional? Actually positional is relative to average at position, so it depends on definition. Often: - For position players, WAA uses batting + baserunning + fielding + positional, all relative to average, then / R/Win. - For pitchers, WAA uses runs above average (based on FIP or RA9) then / R/Win. Then: WAR = WAA + (Replacement Runs / R/Win) WAA is useful for questions like MVP where you want to know how far above average a player was, not just how much playing time they accumulated. WAR includes playing time and replacement credit, which can make durable average players look more valuable than short-season superstars; WAA highlights the “excellence” component.
So if I want to compare peak performance, WAA might be better?

Often yes, especially for partial seasons or when playing time differences are large. But you can also use: - WAR rate (WAR/600 PA, WAR/200 IP) - wRC+ and elite defense metrics - For pitchers, ERA-/FIP- and innings WAA emphasizes how much better than average the player was in the time they played, but it still scales with playing time. It’s not purely a rate stat.
Let’s address a common complaint: “WAR says a DH is less valuable even if he hits like a star.” Why is DH penalized so much?

Because WAR is measuring total value including defense and roster flexibility. A DH provides no defensive value and occupies a lineup spot that could otherwise be used to rest other players or rotate. The positional adjustment for DH (around −17.5 runs per full season) reflects that: - If you can’t field, you’re easier to replace because many players can hit-only, but far fewer can both hit and play a demanding position. - A star DH can still be extremely valuable; his bat can overcome the penalty. But he needs to hit more than a comparable 1B/OF to provide the same total value. This aligns with how teams pay and roster-build: elite two-way contributors (hit + field) are more scarce.
How do they handle first basemen? They also get a penalty, but they do field.

First base has a negative positional adjustment (often around −12.5 runs per 1350 innings) because: - Defensive difficulty is lower than middle infield/CF/C. - The pool of competent 1B defenders is larger. - Many players can move to 1B as they age. But 1B defense still matters: fielding runs can offset some of the positional penalty if a 1B is an excellent defender. The positional adjustment is about baseline difficulty, not saying “defense at 1B doesn’t count.”
Is the positional adjustment “double counting” since fielding runs already compare within position?

It’s not double counting; it’s adjusting between positions. - Fielding runs compare you to average at your position. - Positional adjustment compares the average difficulty/value of that position relative to others. Without positional adjustment, an average-fielding shortstop and an average-fielding first baseman with identical offense would have identical WAR, which would imply positions are equally scarce/difficult. But in roster construction, they are not. So you need both: - Within-position fielding evaluation (fielding runs) - Between-position scarcity/difficulty (positional adjustment)
I want to understand the “defensive spectrum” concept. Is it empirical or just a heuristic?

It started as a heuristic (C/SS/2B/CF/3B/RF/LF/1B/DH), but it’s supported empirically by: - Where players move as they age or lose mobility (SS to 3B/2B, CF to corner OF, etc.) - Offensive expectations by position - Observed defensive difficulty in terms of range/throwing/handling Modern positional adjustments are more empirical than early versions, but the basic ordering remains. There’s debate about exact values (e.g., CF vs 2B vs 3B), and the spectrum can shift with strategy (shifts, pitching changes), but the idea is still useful.
Let’s do an example of a player who plays 1B and hits well. How good does he need to be offensively to be a 5 WAR player?

Roughly, to reach 5 WAR as a 1B, you need to overcome the positional penalty and still provide lots of runs. Assume: - Full season: 650 PA, 1350 innings at 1B - Positional adjustment: −12.5 runs - Replacement runs: +20 runs - Fielding: say +0 (average) - Baserunning: say +0 R/Win: 10 To get 5 WAR, you need ~50 runs above replacement. RAR = Batting + 0 + 0 − 12.5 + 20 = Batting + 7.5 Set RAR = 50: Batting ≈ 42.5 runs above average 42.5 batting runs above average over 650 PA is strong—roughly a wRAA in the low 40s. That corresponds to something like: wRAA = ((wOBA − lgwOBA)/scale)*PA If scale=1.25, PA=650, need wOBA difference: wOBA − lgwOBA = wRAA * scale / PA = 42.5*1.25/650 ≈ 0.0817 If lg wOBA .320, player wOBA ≈ .402 That’s MVP-level hitting. If the 1B also has +10 fielding runs and +3 baserunning, the required wOBA drops. So 5 WAR at 1B is doable but requires star offense unless you’re also an elite defender/runner.
This highlights why corner positions have fewer 6-7 WAR seasons unless the bat is insane.

Exactly. The positional adjustment makes it harder for low-defense positions to pile up WAR unless they’re historically great hitters or add value in other ways. Conversely, a shortstop with strong defense can reach 5–6 WAR with merely very good (not elite) offense.
How do you think about WAR when comparing players across positions for awards like MVP?

WAR is a good starting point because it puts everyone on one scale. Best practice for awards discussions: 1) Use WAR as a summary, not the whole argument Look at: - Offense (wRC+), including park/league context - Defense (multiple metrics if possible) - Baserunning - Playing time and positional value 2) Check multiple WAR versions If fWAR and bWAR agree within, say, 0.5–1.0, that’s strong. If not, investigate why. 3) Consider uncertainty and narrative carefully If two players are within ~0.5 WAR, it’s basically a tie given uncertainty. Then you can reasonably use other considerations: team context, clutch (if you care), etc., while acknowledging that “clutch” is not reliably repeatable. 4) Don’t overinterpret decimals WAR to one decimal is already more precise than the underlying components.
Are there known cases where fWAR and bWAR differ by like 3 WAR for a position player? What causes that?

Yes, especially for players with extreme defensive valuations or catcher framing differences. A 3 WAR gap is large but can happen when: - One system rates a player as +20 defense and the other as 0 or negative (a 20+ run swing is ~2 WAR). - Catcher framing is included differently, adding another 10–20 runs. - Positioning/shift impacts are handled differently. - Positional splits differ (one system credits more innings at a premium position). - Park factor differences and batting runs differences add smaller increments. So large gaps usually trace back to defense and catching.
If I want to build my own WAR model, what are the minimal ingredients I need?

Minimal viable WAR for position players: 1) Offensive runs above average: - Use wOBA/wRAA or linear weights from event data. 2) Baserunning runs: - Could start with SB/CS runs only (simpler) or include advancement (harder). 3) Defensive runs: - Use an existing metric (OAA, DRS, UZR) converted to runs. 4) Positional adjustment: - Apply standard constants prorated by innings. 5) Replacement level: - Choose a baseline runs/PA below average and scale by PA. 6) Park and league adjustments: - At minimum, park-adjust offense; for defense, it’s less critical; for pitching, park matters. 7) Runs-to-wins conversion: - Use season-specific R/Win (or approximate 10). For pitchers: - Decide FIP-based or RA9-based. - Compute runs above average over IP. - Add replacement. - Convert to wins. Then validate by checking: - League totals sum to expected WAR - Team WAR correlates with team wins - Player distributions look reasonable
For offense, if I only have slash line stats (AVG/OBP/SLG) and not event breakdown, can I approximate WAR?

You can approximate, but accuracy drops. Options: - Use OPS+ or wRC+ if available and then estimate batting runs from it: Batting Runs Above Average ≈ ( (wRC+ − 100) / 100 ) * (lgR/PA) * PA Where lgR/PA is league runs per PA (~0.11–0.12 in modern MLB). This is rough because wRC+ already includes park/league adjustments. If you only have OBP/SLG: - You can approximate wOBA via a linear combination of OBP and SLG, but it’s not great. - Better is to reconstruct events if you have at least BB, HBP, HR, etc. Defense and baserunning will be the bigger missing pieces anyway, so a slash-line-only WAR would be heavily offense-weighted and incomplete.
That makes sense. Let’s go back to the pitching side. How does fWAR handle pitchers in extreme parks like Coors?

FanGraphs FIP-based WAR park-adjusts by using park factors in the conversion between FIP and runs allowed context. Because FIP includes HR, and parks affect HR rates and run scoring, they adjust the baseline so a pitcher in a hitter-friendly park isn’t unfairly penalized. Mechanically: - FIP itself uses a constant tied to league average ERA; if you compute FIP within a league/season, it’s already in that context. - But to compare pitchers across parks, you apply park factors to the pitcher’s FIP or to the league baseline used in runs above average. In practice, FanGraphs includes park adjustments in pitcher WAR calculations, though the exact formula is detailed in their methodology. The goal is: a 4.00 FIP in Coors is more valuable than a 4.00 FIP in a pitcher park, because it’s harder to achieve. bWAR’s RA9 approach also park-adjusts runs allowed.
One more subtlety: innings pitched vs batters faced. Some models use batters faced. Why?

Using batters faced (BF) can be more granular because: - Not all innings are equal in terms of opportunities; an inning can have 3 batters or 10 batters. - For FIP components (K, BB, HR), BF is the natural denominator. However, runs allowed and ERA/FIP are traditionally per 9 innings, and IP is widely available and stable. WAR systems often use IP for simplicity and convention. In RA9-based models, IP is also the standard because runs allowed are tied to innings. In FIP-based models, you could compute runs above average using BF and convert to runs using per-BF rates, but most public WAR sticks to IP/9 scaling for interpretability.
How does the “constant” in FIP ensure league average FIP equals league average ERA, but WAR is about runs, not earned runs?

Right: ERA is earned runs; RA9 is total runs. FIP is designed to match ERA scale, not RA9. That’s one conceptual mismatch if you think in terms of total runs. FanGraphs pitcher WAR uses FIP as an estimator of runs allowed attributable to the pitcher, but it’s essentially on an earned-run scale. The WAR conversion uses league average FIP and replacement level defined in that same FIP framework, so internally it’s consistent. If you want total runs, you’d use RA9-WAR. bWAR uses runs allowed and then adjusts for defense, which keeps it closer to total runs. So: FIP-WAR is consistent within its own model but doesn’t literally say “this many actual runs prevented.” It says “this many runs prevented as estimated by FIP relative to replacement.”
That’s helpful. Now, can you explain why a pitcher with a low ERA but mediocre FIP might still be truly skilled? Not just luck.

There are plausible skills beyond the “three true outcomes”: 1) Contact quality suppression Pitchers may influence exit velocity, launch angle, and weak contact rates. Modern Statcast metrics (xwOBA allowed, barrel rate) suggest some pitchers consistently induce weaker contact. 2) Groundball tendency Groundballs are less likely to become extra-base hits. FIP doesn’t directly credit groundball skill except indirectly through HR suppression. 3) Sequencing skill (debated) Some pitchers may have repeatable ability to pitch better with runners on (stretch performance), though evidence suggests most sequencing is noisy. But certain pitch arsenals might play up in high leverage. 4) Pitcher-fielding and holding runners These can reduce runs without showing in FIP. 5) Team/catcher effects Catcher framing, game-calling, and pitch sequencing can affect outcomes. A pitcher paired with a great framer might beat FIP in RA9/ERA. So “beating FIP” can be skill + context + luck. Over many seasons, persistent gaps might indicate real skill (or persistent context), but single-season gaps are often luck.
Does WAR incorporate Statcast expected stats for pitchers yet?

Standard public WAR versions generally do not use Statcast expected stats (xERA, xwOBA) as the core. They remain FIP-based (FanGraphs) or RA9-based (Baseball-Reference). Statcast expected metrics are available and useful for analysis and projection, but WAR providers prioritize continuity and transparent components. That said, analysts often use: - fWAR for a baseline - plus Statcast indicators to evaluate whether the WAR is “deserved” or likely to persist
If WAR is a framework, could we define a “contact-WAR” for pitchers using xwOBA allowed?

You could. Outline: 1) Convert xwOBA allowed into expected runs allowed per BF or per 9 using a run estimator (similar to converting wOBA to wRAA). 2) Compare pitcher expected runs to league average, adjust for park, scale by BF or IP. 3) Add replacement runs. 4) Convert to wins. Challenges: - xwOBA allowed includes K/BB/contact quality, but mapping it to runs allowed requires calibration and may double count some aspects if not careful. - It’s dependent on Statcast model assumptions and can shift with model updates. - It doesn’t naturally include sequencing; it’s an estimator. But yes, conceptually it’s feasible and might be more predictive than FIP in some cases.
Let’s shift to the philosophical question: Why does baseball analytics like WAR so much compared to other sports?

Baseball is unusually amenable to decomposition: - Discrete events: plate appearances and batted balls are separable units with relatively clear responsibility. - Low interaction compared to continuous-flow sports: while context matters, you can isolate player contributions more cleanly. - Rich historical data: decades of box score data, plus modern tracking. - Scoring structure: runs are additive, and run expectancy models map events to run values. Other sports have more complex interactions and fewer discrete, independently attributable events, making “one-number value” harder and more model-dependent. WAR isn’t perfect, but baseball’s structure makes it more feasible than, say, soccer.
But even in baseball, defense attribution seems hard. Are there new approaches that could reduce model dependence?

Statcast tracking has improved defense attribution substantially by modeling: - Starting position - Reaction time - Sprint speed - Route efficiency - Hang time for fly balls - Throw speed and accuracy (for arm value) OAA and related metrics can separate “range” from “arm” more cleanly. Over time, more sophisticated models can: - Account for team positioning strategy (who was responsible for which zone) - Incorporate pitcher tendencies (spray distributions) - Use hierarchical models to stabilize estimates That could reduce disagreement and noise. But any model still involves choices about responsibility and run conversion.
I’d like to understand how the run conversion for defense works. Like, if a fielder makes an out on a ball that’s usually a single, how many runs is that worth?

Defense run values typically depend on: - The change in expected runs due to turning a hit into an out (or vice versa). - The base/out state and the type of hit prevented. In simplified terms: - Preventing a single is worth less than preventing a double or triple because extra bases increase run expectancy more. - Turning a ball in play into an out also adds an out, which is valuable. Many defensive metrics use average run values by batted-ball type/location: - If a ball to a certain zone becomes an out X% of the time, the fielder gets credit for converting it. The difference between out and hit is assigned a run value based on average consequences. Example (illustrative): - If converting a would-be single into an out reduces run expectancy by ~0.5 runs on average, then making that play earns +0.5 runs relative to average. If an average fielder makes it 70% and you make it 80%, across 100 opportunities you gain 10 extra outs; 10 * 0.5 = +5 runs. Real models use more granular values and account for distribution of base/out states.
Does WAR incorporate “clutch defense” or high leverage fielding plays?

Standard WAR does not; it uses context-neutral defensive value. A great play in the 9th inning of a tie game is valued similarly to the same play in the 2nd inning. There are context-dependent defensive measures (like defensive WPA), but they are not part of WAR. This is consistent with WAR’s philosophy: measure underlying contribution independent of leverage usage, which can be partly luck and opportunity-driven.
How does WAR handle errors? Are they treated as negative defense?

Yes. Defensive metrics include error components, but they treat them relative to league average error rates and opportunities. - An error is effectively a failure to record an out, sometimes allowing extra bases. It increases run expectancy. - UZR includes an “ErrR” component for errors beyond expected. - DRS includes misplays and errors as part of its saved runs. However, modern metrics also recognize that some players with great range commit more errors because they reach more balls. Good defensive models attempt to separate range from hands/throwing so you don’t punish a player for getting to balls others don’t reach. But scoring and classification can still be messy.
I’ve seen “UZR/150.” How does that relate to UZR in WAR?

UZR is a counting stat in runs, based on actual innings played. UZR/150 is a rate stat scaled to 150 games (or ~1350 innings), intended to represent what the player would be worth defensively over a full season at that position. In WAR, you use UZR (the counting runs) because WAR is counting value. If you only have UZR/150, you can convert: UZR ≈ (UZR/150) * (Innings / 1350) Then add it to other components.
For someone learning sabermetrics, what are the common pitfalls in interpreting WAR?

Common pitfalls: 1) Treating WAR as exact Differences under ~0.5 WAR are often noise. 2) Ignoring component differences If two WAR versions disagree, don’t just argue the top-line number; inspect defense and pitcher model differences. 3) Confusing descriptive and predictive WAR is descriptive; projections are predictive. 4) Overvaluing single-season defense Defense needs multi-year context. 5) Not adjusting for playing time WAR is total value; rate stats matter too. 6) Comparing across eras without acknowledging data limitations Especially for defense and catching. 7) Using WAR as the only argument It’s a summary; context and component stats matter.
Can you explain why WAR often correlates with salary but not perfectly?

Salaries reflect: - Expected future performance (not just past) - Aging curves and injury risk - Market dynamics and positional scarcity - Contract timing (free agency vs arbitration) - Team budget constraints and preferences WAR correlates with salary because teams pay for wins, but imperfections arise because: - WAR is backward-looking; contracts are forward-looking. - Players in arbitration are underpaid relative to WAR. - Defense and pitching models differ from team internal valuations. - Star power and revenue effects can add value beyond on-field WAR (not captured). So WAR is a strong but not perfect predictor of compensation.
If I wanted to translate WAR into dollars, what’s the typical $/WAR?

In free agency markets in recent years, estimates often range around $8M to $10M per WAR, varying by offseason and inflation. But it’s not constant: - Marginal wins are more valuable for contenders (playoff odds). - Elite players may be paid differently due to scarcity and risk. - Long-term deals include aging decline and risk. A common approach: Market value ≈ WAR * $/WAR Then adjust for: - Aging projection - Discount rate - Injury risk - Contract structure But $/WAR is an approximation, not a law.
Back to the math: how do you handle park factors precisely? Are they symmetric for hitters and pitchers?

Park factors aim to measure how a park affects run scoring or specific events relative to a neutral park, controlling for team quality and schedule. They can be: - Runs-based park factors: compare runs scored/allowed in home vs road games. - Component park factors: HR factor, doubles factor, etc. Symmetry: - A runs park factor affects both hitters and pitchers. A hitter-friendly park inflates both team offense and opponent offense at that park. - For individual player evaluation, you want to neutralize performance: a hitter’s raw stats in a hitter park are deflated to neutral; a pitcher’s run prevention in a hitter park is credited more. Precise implementation: - Use multi-year park factors to reduce noise. - Regress park factors toward 1.00 (neutral) because they’re noisy. - Apply different factors to different components if you’re doing component-based models. FanGraphs and Baseball-Reference have their own park factor methodologies, which is another source of small WAR differences.
Is there an example where park factor differences alone could change WAR by a full win?

It’s uncommon but possible for extreme cases: - A player with huge home/road split in an extreme park (e.g., Coors) and lots of playing time. - If one system uses a different park factor magnitude or multi-year window, the neutralized batting runs could shift by, say, 8–12 runs, which is close to 1 WAR. Usually, park factor differences are smaller and contribute maybe a few runs. Defense and pitcher model differences are more common sources of 1+ WAR gaps.
You mentioned that WAR is calibrated so league totals sum to about 990 WAR. Does each system actually sum to that?

Roughly, yes, but not perfectly. Differences occur because: - They may use different replacement win% assumptions. - They may treat pitchers and position players separately with different replacement baselines, and totals might not align exactly when summed. - Interleague play, unbalanced schedules, and league adjustments complicate the accounting. - Rounding and data corrections. But as a sanity check, total WAR across MLB in a season should be in the neighborhood of ~900–1,100 depending on assumptions. If a homebrew model yields 1,500 WAR, something is off.
How do they split WAR between pitchers and fielders for run prevention? Like, what fraction of team WAR is pitching?

It varies by season and the model. - In FIP-WAR (FanGraphs), pitchers get credit for K/BB/HR outcomes and are insulated from defense. That tends to allocate a lot of run prevention value to pitchers based on peripherals. - In RA9-based models, more of the observed run prevention is initially attributed to pitchers, then defense adjustments may redistribute some. At the team level, you’ll often see roughly: - Position players: maybe ~55–60% of total WAR - Pitchers: maybe ~40–45% But it can vary. Because a team’s offense and defense are both crucial, and pitchers are a big part of defense/run prevention, it’s not surprising that pitchers account for a large share.
Is there a WAR for managers?

Not a standard one. Manager impact is difficult to quantify because: - Lineup decisions and bullpen usage are constrained by roster quality. - Many decisions are not observable (clubhouse, preparation). - In-game tactics have relatively small run expectancy impacts compared to player talent.
