Some studies estimate manager value in wins using models of lineup optimization and bullpen leverage, but it’s not robust enough for a widely accepted “manager WAR.”
Another debate: should WAR include postseason?

Standard WAR is regular season only. Reasons: - Postseason sample sizes are tiny; variance dominates. - Opponent quality is higher; context differs. - WAR is calibrated to regular-season replacement and run environments. You can compute postseason WPA or add postseason WAR-like value, but it’s not standard. For legacy discussions, some people separately cite postseason performance.
I’m trying to build intuition: what does 1 WAR “look like” in terms of performance?

Rough translations (very approximate): For hitters: - +10 runs above replacement ≈ 1 WAR (if R/Win ~10). Since replacement is ~20 runs below average per full season, 1 WAR might be: - A slightly below-average regular (say, -10 runs vs average overall) who plays full time. Or: - A part-time player who’s quite good per PA but only plays half a season. In batting terms: - 1 WAR could be ~+10 batting runs above average if defense/position are neutral and you have full playing time, but because replacement credit is included, the mapping depends on playing time and position. For pitchers: - 1 WAR might be ~10 runs prevented relative to replacement, e.g., a starter with ~50 IP of slightly better-than-replacement performance, or a reliever with excellent performance in ~60 IP. A rough heuristic: - 2 WAR = solid starter/regular - 4 WAR = All-Star level - 6 WAR = MVP/Cy Young candidate - 8+ WAR = elite, rare season But position and role matter.
How does WAR treat pitchers with many unearned runs? bWAR uses runs allowed; does it penalize them?

bWAR uses runs allowed (RA) which includes unearned runs, but it also considers the source of those runs and team defense adjustments. If a pitcher allows many unearned runs due to errors behind him, that could be partly adjusted away via defense adjustment. However, if the pitcher contributes to errors (e.g., poor fielding, inducing difficult plays) it’s complicated. FanGraphs FIP-WAR ignores unearned runs entirely because it doesn’t use runs allowed; it uses FIP components. So a pitcher with many unearned runs will look worse in RA9 measures than in FIP measures, unless defense adjustments compensate.
Does WAR account for pitcher fielding (PFP) and holding runners?

Generally, not explicitly in standard WAR. Some components might be partially captured: - Holding runners affects stolen bases and advancement; that can show up in catcher/runner metrics more than pitcher metrics. - Pitcher fielding can affect team defense and unearned runs, but it’s rarely isolated. There are advanced metrics like: - Pitcher Defensive Runs Saved (for fielding bunts, come-backers, etc.) - Stolen base prevention metrics attributed to pitchers These are small in magnitude for most pitchers, but could matter at the margins. They’re usually not included in headline WAR.
What about “pitch framing” being credited to catchers. Doesn’t that affect pitcher WAR too?

It affects pitcher results (called strikes change counts, Ks, BBs, etc.). In FIP-WAR: - If framing increases strikeouts and decreases walks, it improves the pitcher’s FIP and thus increases fWAR, even though the catcher contributed. In RA9-WAR: - Framing can reduce runs by improving outcomes, increasing pitcher WAR. This is an attribution problem: who deserves the credit, pitcher or catcher? Some frameworks attempt to split framing value: - Give framing runs to catchers (as in catcher defense metrics) - Adjust pitcher metrics to remove framing effects (rare in public WAR) Most public WAR doesn’t fully separate these, so some catcher value may be indirectly credited to pitchers, and vice versa. This is one reason catcher and pitcher valuations can be contentious.
That seems like a big issue for teams with elite framers.

It can be, especially in the era when framing was large and unevenly distributed. If you’re using WAR to evaluate pitchers, you should consider catcher context: - Compare pitcher performance with different catchers - Look at called-strike metrics - Use models that attempt to control for catcher framing if available For public WAR, it’s a known limitation.
Let’s talk about batted-ball luck and BABIP. How does that factor into WAR differences?

BABIP (batting average on balls in play) influences: - Pitcher RA9/ERA strongly - FIP not at all (except indirectly through HR, which are not in play) So: - A pitcher with very low BABIP will have lower RA9 than FIP suggests and thus higher bWAR than fWAR. - A pitcher with high BABIP will look worse in RA9 and thus lower bWAR than fWAR. Whether that’s “luck” or “skill” is debated: - Some pitchers have persistent BABIP suppression via weak contact, pop-ups, etc. - But much of year-to-year BABIP variation is noise. WAR differences are partly reflecting that philosophical split.
For hitters, is there an equivalent to BABIP luck affecting WAR?

Yes, to some extent: - wOBA is based on actual outcomes (hits, HR, etc.). If a hitter has unusually high or low BABIP, his wOBA and thus batting runs will reflect that. - But for hitters, BABIP is more skill-influenced than for pitchers (hitters control quality of contact more directly). Still, there’s luck in where balls land. WAR is descriptive, so it credits the results. If you want to separate luck, you’d look at: - xwOBA, expected BA, etc. - Contact quality metrics But WAR itself doesn’t adjust for “luck” in batting beyond being context-neutral.
Can you explain the relationship between WAR and run differential? Like, if a team has 40 WAR, what run differential does that correspond to?

Roughly: Team WAR * R/Win ≈ team runs above replacement. But run differential is relative to average opponents, not replacement. If replacement level is ~48 wins, then a 40 WAR team is ~88 wins. A typical 88-win team might have a run differential around +80 to +100, depending on sequencing and one-run games. In terms of runs: 40 WAR * 10 runs/win ≈ 400 runs above replacement. But replacement itself is below average. If replacement is ~20 runs per 600 PA per player and ~something for pitchers, the “above replacement” runs include a large baseline. So mapping WAR directly to run differential is not straightforward. WAA (wins above average) maps more directly: WAA * R/Win ≈ runs above average, which is closer to run differential.
So WAA is more directly tied to run differential and Pythagorean wins?

Yes. If you sum player runs above average (not including replacement) on offense and pitching/defense, you get something closer to “team runs above average,” which corresponds more directly to run differential and Pythagorean wins. WAR adds the replacement baseline, which shifts the zero point.
I want to understand how WAR treats pinch hitters and defensive replacements. Do they get positional adjustments?

Positional adjustment is based on defensive innings, so: - A pinch hitter who never fields gets DH-like treatment if he has no position? Some systems treat him as a DH for positional adjustment purposes when he has no defensive innings, or they may assign a “PH” position with a penalty similar to DH. - A defensive replacement who plays few innings gets a prorated positional adjustment based on those innings, plus his defensive runs in that small sample (very noisy). WAR is playing-time sensitive, so bench roles usually produce small WAR totals unless the player is extremely effective in limited opportunities.
Does WAR credit platoon advantage? Like, if a player is used optimally vs LHP only.

WAR credits actual outcomes. If a platoon hitter posts a high wOBA in limited PA because he’s sheltered from same-side matchups, WAR will credit him for that performance, but because PA are limited, total WAR may still be modest. WAR doesn’t explicitly add value for managerial optimization; it just records what happened. For evaluating true talent, you’d consider that platoon splits may regress and that the player might not perform as well with full-time usage.
Ok. Let’s do a long example comparing two players: one is a great hitter at 1B with poor defense, the other is a good hitter at SS with great defense. Show how WAR can rank them similarly.

Let’s set: Season constants: - R/Win = 10 - Replacement runs: +20 for full-time 650 PA Positional adjustments: - 1B: −12.5 - SS: +7.5 Player A (1B): - PA 650 - Batting: +45 runs - Baserunning: −2 - Fielding: −10 - Position: −12.5 - Replacement: +20 RAR = 45 − 2 − 10 − 12.5 + 20 = 40.5 WAR = 4.05 Player B (SS): - PA 650 - Batting: +20 runs - Baserunning: +3 - Fielding: +15 - Position: +7.5 - Replacement: +20 RAR = 20 + 3 + 15 + 7.5 + 20 = 65.5 WAR = 6.55 Here B is clearly higher because we made defense big. But adjust to make them similar: If Player A is an even better hitter: - Batting +65 runs (MVP bat) Then A: 65 −2 −10 −12.5 +20 = 60.5 => 6.05 WAR Now they’re similar: A at 6.1 WAR, B at 6.6 WAR. That illustrates: - A must hit at an elite level to offset poor defense and low positional value. - B can be “only” very good offensively but elite defensively at SS and still match. This is the core of WAR’s positional logic.
This is good. Now, can you explain why some fans dislike WAR? What are the legitimate critiques?

Legitimate critiques include: 1) Defensive uncertainty Defense is hard to measure; different systems disagree; single-season values are noisy. 2) Pitcher model disagreement FIP vs RA9 leads to very different conclusions; neither is “the truth” for all purposes. 3) Framing/positioning attribution Credit allocation among catcher/pitcher/fielders is imperfect. 4) Black-box perception Even though methods are published, the calculations are complex and can feel opaque. 5) Overuse as a trump card People sometimes end debates by citing WAR without acknowledging uncertainty or context. 6) Context and role WAR is context-neutral; some people care about clutch, leverage, postseason, etc., which WAR intentionally excludes. These critiques don’t mean WAR is useless—just that it should be used appropriately.
Let’s get more technical on converting wRC+ to batting runs. You gave a rough formula. Can you derive it?

Sure, in simplified form. Let: - wRC+ = 100 * (player R/PA) / (lg R/PA), after park adjustment. So: player R/PA = (wRC+ / 100) * lg R/PA Runs created above average per PA: (player R/PA − lg R/PA) = (wRC+/100 − 1) * lg R/PA Total runs above average: Batting Runs Above Avg ≈ (wRC+/100 − 1) * lg R/PA * PA Because wRC+ is already park/league adjusted, this gives you a park/league neutral batting run estimate. Caveats: - wRC+ is based on wOBA and includes some scaling; it’s not exactly “runs created” in a literal accounting sense, but it’s close. - You’d want to use the proper league R/PA constant used in the wRC framework. This derivation is why wRC+ is convenient: it directly indexes run creation rate.
How does WAR treat mid-season trades? Does league context change?

Yes. If a player changes leagues or parks mid-season, WAR calculations typically: - Use the appropriate league context for each portion of playing time. - Apply park factors based on home parks played in. - For batting, park adjustments can be applied at the player-season level with weighted park factors based on games played in each park. Both FanGraphs and Baseball-Reference handle this, though details differ. The goal is: performance is normalized appropriately given where it occurred.
Does WAR consider strength of schedule? Like facing tougher pitching in one division?

Not directly. WAR adjusts for league and park, not opponent quality. Over a full season, schedule differences exist but are usually small compared to noise and are not included in standard WAR. For more precise evaluation, you could incorporate opponent quality, but that becomes complex and can introduce circularity (opponent quality depends on the same player evaluations).
Are there any “contextual WAR” models that incorporate leverage or opponent?

There are models like: - WPA (win probability added): fully contextual, includes leverage and opponent in a sense because game state includes score/inning. - RE24: contextual to base/out state. - Championship WPA: postseason leverage. But these aren’t WAR; they answer “how much did you change win probability given situations you were in?” rather than “how good were you in a context-neutral sense?” Some people compute “WPA-based WAR” or “contextual wins,” but it’s not standard and tends to be less stable.
If I were teaching WAR to someone, what sequence of concepts would you introduce?

A good sequence: 1) Runs and wins Explain that wins correlate with run differential; marginal runs translate to marginal wins. 2) Linear weights for offense Introduce run expectancy and event run values; define wOBA/wRAA. 3) Replacement level Explain why average ≠ baseline; define replacement and why playing time matters. 4) Defense in runs Explain converting outs/hits prevented to runs; introduce UZR/DRS/OAA and uncertainty. 5) Positional adjustment Explain scarcity/difficulty and why SS/C get bonuses, 1B/DH penalties. 6) Put it together RAR sum and divide by R/Win. 7) Variants and disagreements Explain fWAR vs bWAR, especially for pitchers and defense. 8) Interpretation Error bars, descriptive vs predictive, use cases.
I want to understand the difference between “runs above average” and “runs above replacement” in a more formal way. Can you define the baselines?

Formally: Let player performance be measured in runs relative to some baseline. Define: - Average baseline: the mean performance of players at a given position/role, in that league/season context. By definition, average is 0 runs above average. - Replacement baseline: a lower baseline representing freely available talent. Replacement is a fixed offset below average, typically defined so that a team of replacement players would win ~0.294 of games. Then: Runs Above Replacement (RAR) = Runs Above Average (RAA) + (Average − Replacement) * playing_time_rate Where (Average − Replacement) is a positive number of runs per PA or per IP. It’s the “replacement runs” credit. So RAA measures how good you were relative to average; RAR measures value relative to replacement, which is the relevant baseline for roster decisions (because you can always choose to use a replacement player instead).
Is replacement level constant across time? Or does it change with league talent?

It’s defined relative to league average each season, so it shifts with the league. If the league gets better, replacement should also get better, but remain a fixed distance below average in wins% terms. However, the distribution of talent can change with expansion, integration, international recruitment, etc. A fixed .294 baseline may not perfectly track true “freely available” talent across history. So replacement level is partly a convention, which is another reason cross-era comparisons have uncertainty. Still, because WAR is relative to league context, it’s reasonably robust for broad comparisons.
How do WAR systems treat expansion eras? Does the baseline shift?

They keep replacement defined relative to league average in that season. Expansion can dilute talent and change the average level, but WAR is anchored to that season’s league average. If replacement is a fixed offset from average, then in an expansion year where average pitching/hitting changes, replacement shifts accordingly. The bigger issue is that some components (like defense) may be harder to evaluate in older eras, not that replacement is mis-set.
Can we talk about “WAR for minor leaguers” or translating minor league performance into WAR?

That’s usually done via projections, not direct WAR, because minor league stats occur in different run environments and competition levels. Approach: 1) Translate minor league performance to MLB equivalents (MLEs) using league difficulty factors. 2) Project to MLB using aging curves and regression. 3) Convert projected MLB performance to runs and then WAR with the same framework. So you can talk about “future WAR” or “projected WAR” for prospects, but it’s model-based and uncertain.
How does WAR handle “replacement-level” for a prospect who is called up? They aren’t freely available in the same way.

WAR’s replacement level is not about the individual player’s cost or availability; it’s a baseline for value. A prospect can be well above replacement immediately; WAR credits that. Economic concepts (like surplus value) are separate: - A prospect might have high WAR but low salary due to team control, creating surplus value. WAR is about on-field wins, not contracts.
That’s clear. Let’s do a sanity check: If an average everyday player is ~2 WAR, does that mean a team of average players would be 48 + 2*9 = 66 wins? That seems wrong.

The confusion is mixing “average everyday player” with “average roster spot.” A team has: - 9 lineup spots, but not all are filled by 650-PA everyday players; there are bench players, platoons, injuries. - Pitching staff contributes a large share of WAR too. Also, the “average full-time position player” being ~2 WAR is a rule of thumb; the exact number depends on position and playing time. A team of average players (average at each role) should win ~81 games, not 66. In WAR terms: - Average team WAR is roughly total wins above replacement: 81 − 48 = 33 WAR (if replacement is 48 wins). So an average team has about 33 WAR total (hitters + pitchers). That implies the “average” roster, when weighted by playing time, sums to 33 WAR, not 2*9. Because you have more than 9 contributors, and pitchers add a lot. So: 2 WAR is for a full-time regular, not the average of all players on a roster.
Great point. How many players typically accumulate non-trivial WAR on a team?

Often: - 12–16 position players who get significant PA/innings - 12–20 pitchers who get significant innings But the distribution is skewed: - A few stars accumulate large WAR - Many players contribute small fractions - Some are negative So team WAR totals can be decomposed among ~25–35 players with playing time.
Now, can you explain why WAR can differ depending on whether you use innings or games at position for positional adjustment?

Positional adjustment is proportional to defensive playing time. Using innings is more precise because: - Defensive innings capture partial games and substitutions. - Games started can misrepresent time (a player might start but leave early). If a system uses games or games started as a proxy, it can slightly misallocate positional runs, especially for players who frequently switch positions mid-game. Most modern WAR uses innings, but historical data might require approximations. Small differences here can change WAR by a few tenths for multi-position players.
Is there a similar issue for catchers with “catcher handling” or staff effects?

Yes. Catcher value is multifaceted: - Framing (quantifiable) - Blocking (quantifiable) - Throwing (quantifiable) - Game-calling / pitcher performance (hard to isolate) - Pitcher-catcher interaction effects Older stats like “CERA” (catcher ERA) attempted to measure staff handling, but it’s confounded by pitcher quality and small samples. Modern research suggests catcher game-calling effects exist but are smaller than framing and hard to estimate reliably. Public WAR largely avoids attributing large “handling” value beyond framing and blocking/throwing, because of identification challenges.
If I’m comparing two players and one has 7.1 fWAR and 6.2 bWAR, is it meaningful?

It’s meaningful as a signal that the models disagree by ~0.9 WAR, which is not trivial but within the range where defense/pitching model uncertainty can explain it. Best practice: - Look at the breakdown: - If hitter: compare UZR vs DRS, positional splits, baserunning. - If pitcher: compare FIP vs RA9, defense/park context. - Consider multi-year context. - Treat the difference as uncertainty, not as evidence one is “wrong.” If you need a single number, you could cite a range: “about 6–7 WAR.”
Are there players for whom fWAR is systematically higher than bWAR over many years?

Yes, patterns can emerge: Pitchers: - High-strikeout, low-walk pitchers with higher ERAs (due to sequencing or HR clustering) might have higher fWAR than bWAR. - Pitchers who consistently allow fewer runs than FIP suggests might have higher bWAR. Hitters: - Players whose defense is rated higher by UZR than DRS (or vice versa) might show systematic gaps. - Catchers depending on framing treatment. But “systematic” can also reflect measurement bias, not necessarily true skill differences.
How do you decide which defensive metric is better, UZR or DRS?

There’s no definitive answer. Ways to evaluate: 1) Predictive validity Does the metric predict future defensive outcomes better than alternatives (after controlling for playing time)? This is tricky because defense itself is noisy. 2) Convergence with Statcast In recent years, compare to OAA and other tracking-based measures. But those are also models. 3) Face validity and component logic Does it handle positioning, arm, double plays, etc., sensibly? 4) Stability and year-to-year correlation Too much volatility suggests noise, but too much stability could suggest over-smoothing. Many analysts use a blend: consider multiple metrics and regress.
How does WAR treat league-average defense at a position? Is it set to zero?

Defensive runs are measured relative to average at that position, so the average fielder at a position is around 0 defensive runs (by construction). But because of playing time weighting and measurement, the sum across all fielders at a position should be near zero. Then positional adjustment shifts the baseline across positions.
I’ve also seen “Rpos” and “Rfield” on Baseball-Reference. Are those the positional and fielding components?

Yes, broadly: - Rfield: fielding runs (from DRS and related components) - Rpos: positional adjustment in runs - Rbat: batting runs - Rbaser: baserunning runs - Rdp: double-play runs (sometimes separated) - Replacement runs: sometimes implicit in the WAR calculation Then they sum to runs above replacement and divide by runs per win to get WAR. FanGraphs uses: - Bat: batting runs (from wRAA) - BsR: baserunning - Field: UZR (or similar) - Pos: positional - Rep: replacement Then / R/Win.
Why does Baseball-Reference separate double-play runs?

Because grounding into double plays is partly a function of speed and batted-ball profile, and they treat it as a distinct component. FanGraphs includes GDP avoidance in baserunning (wGDP). Baseball-Reference may separate it as Rdp. It’s bookkeeping; the goal is to capture the run impact of avoiding or hitting into double plays relative to average.
Is grounding into double plays more a batting skill or a baserunning skill?

It’s mixed: - Batter speed affects whether he’s doubled up. - Batter batted-ball tendency (ground balls) affects opportunities. - Situational context (runner on first, <2 outs) is not fully controlled by the batter. Because it’s partly about running speed and partly about batted-ball profile, different systems place it differently. FanGraphs treats it as baserunning because it’s tied to speed/outcome beyond the linear weight of a groundout. Others treat it as batting because it’s an outcome of the plate appearance. Either way, it’s a small component relative to overall offense.
Can you explain why WAR is expressed relative to replacement rather than average? Why not just use WAA?

Replacement is more relevant for roster decisions and total team building: - If you’re deciding whether to sign or play someone, the alternative is often a replacement-level player, not an average player. - WAR aligns with team wins: team wins ≈ replacement wins + team WAR. - WAR gives credit for playing time because filling innings/PA with replacement talent has a cost. WAA is useful for measuring excellence relative to average, but it doesn’t map as cleanly to team construction and marginal value. Both are useful; WAR is more common because it connects naturally to “how many wins did this player add compared to what you could get for free?”
Do you think WAR is best used for historical comparisons like Hall of Fame?

It’s useful, especially for summarizing careers, but with caveats: - Defense and positional adjustments are crucial for comparing players across positions, which is a major Hall of Fame challenge. - But defensive measurement is less reliable historically, so older players’ WAR has more uncertainty. - WAR doesn’t capture postseason (some voters care). - WAR doesn’t capture league/competition changes perfectly. Best practice: - Use WAR as a backbone, but also look at: - Peak vs longevity (7-year peak WAR, JAWS) - Era-adjusted hitting/pitching stats (OPS+, ERA+) - Awards and context as secondary evidence WAR is among the best tools for Hall discussions, but not the only one.
You mentioned JAWS. How does that relate to WAR?

JAWS (Jaffe WAR Score system) uses Baseball-Reference WAR to evaluate Hall of Fame candidacy by combining: - Career WAR - Peak WAR (best 7 seasons, not necessarily consecutive) JAWS = (Career WAR + Peak WAR) / 2 Then compares a player’s JAWS to the average Hall of Famer at his position. It’s a structured way to balance longevity and peak, using WAR as the underlying value metric.
If JAWS uses bWAR, could you do it with fWAR?

You could, but JAWS is traditionally bWAR-based because Baseball-Reference has long historical coverage and consistent position-player WAR series. FanGraphs also has historical WAR, but differences in defensive metrics and availability can complicate. If you compute a FanGraphs-based JAWS, you might get different rankings, especially for defense-heavy players and pitchers (FIP vs RA9). It could be interesting, but you’d want to be consistent and compare to an “average Hall” baseline computed within the same WAR system.
Are there any known systematic differences in how the two systems treat pitcher workloads in the early 1900s?

The biggest issue is data and context: - RA9-based measures can be computed historically from runs allowed, which are recorded. - FIP requires HR, BB, K data, which is incomplete in very early eras; and the relationships between those events and runs may differ. So historically, bWAR is often preferred for early-era pitchers because it uses runs allowed and innings, which are available. FanGraphs has historical pitcher WAR too, but the methodology and data completeness vary. So yes, system differences can be larger historically, and comparisons become more uncertain.
This has been broad. I want to lock in the core reason fWAR and bWAR differ for pitchers: FIP vs RA9. Can you summarize the implications in a crisp way?

Core difference: - fWAR (FanGraphs) for pitchers: values performance based on FIP (K, BB, HBP, HR). It estimates how many runs the pitcher “should” allow given those outcomes, largely stripping defense and sequencing. More predictive, less descriptive. Implication: pitchers who get lots of strikeouts and limit walks/HR rate well will look good, even if their ERA/RA9 was higher. - bWAR (Baseball-Reference) for pitchers: values performance based on actual runs allowed per 9 (RA9), adjusted for park and defense. More descriptive of what happened on the scoreboard. Implication: pitchers who prevented runs (including via contact management, strand rate, or luck) will look good, even if their peripherals are mediocre. So: fWAR answers “how good were his pitcher-controlled outcomes?” bWAR answers “how many runs did he actually allow (after adjustments)?”
For hitters, the core reason is defense metric choice, right?

Yes. For hitters/position players: - Batting and baserunning components are usually close across systems. - The biggest differences come from defense: UZR (FanGraphs) vs DRS (Baseball-Reference), plus how they integrate catcher framing and position splits. So if you see a large fWAR vs bWAR gap for a hitter, check the defensive runs first.
If I want to avoid defense uncertainty, could I compare players using “offensive WAR” only?

You can use: - Batting runs + baserunning + replacement (and possibly positional?) to create an offense-only value. But you must be explicit: - If you exclude positional adjustment, you’re measuring pure offensive contribution above replacement, not total value. - If you include positional adjustment, you’re mixing in positional scarcity, which is not “offense-only.” Common alternatives: - wRAA or wRC (counting runs) - wRC+ (rate) - Offensive runs above average These are excellent for comparing bats, but they won’t tell you who was more valuable overall. If the question is “best hitter,” use those; if “best player,” include defense/position.
One last deep question: Is WAR linear in runs? Baseball isn’t perfectly linear because of diminishing returns in blowouts.

WAR uses a linear approximation: each run is worth the same fraction of a win (via R/Win), regardless of when it occurs. In reality, win probability is nonlinear: runs in blowouts have less marginal win impact. However: - Over large samples and many contexts, linear run-to-win conversion works well as an average. - Using context-neutral linear valuation avoids crediting players for being on teams that created more high-leverage situations. WPA captures the nonlinear, context-dependent value, but it’s opportunity-driven and less stable. So WAR chooses linearity as a practical, interpretable approximation aligned with long-run team building rather than single-game leverage.
Thanks. To close: can you give me a compact checklist for diagnosing why two WAR numbers differ for the same player?

Checklist: 1) Identify player type - Position player or pitcher? 2) If position player: - Compare defensive runs (UZR vs DRS/OAA). Big driver. - Check catcher framing inclusion if catcher. - Check positional innings splits and positional adjustment. - Compare batting runs method (wRAA vs Rbat) and park factors. - Compare baserunning components (BsR vs Rbaser/Rdp). 3) If pitcher: - Is one FIP-based and the other RA9-based? That’s usually the main reason. - Check park factors and league context. - Check defense adjustment (bWAR). - Check role (starter vs reliever) and replacement level assumptions. - Look at sequencing indicators (LOB%, BABIP) to interpret FIP vs RA9 gap. 4) Consider uncertainty - Differences <0.5 WAR often noise; >1 WAR usually traceable to defense or pitcher model choice. If you want, give me a specific player-season and I can walk through the component comparison in detail using the published breakdowns (without needing personal context).
What does PER actually measure and why do some analysts think it's flawed?

Player Efficiency Rating (PER) is John Hollinger’s attempt to collapse a player’s per-minute box-score production into a single number, adjusted for pace and normalized so league average is 15. At a high level, it’s a weighted sum of “good” box-score events (made shots, free throws, assists, rebounds, blocks, steals) minus “bad” ones (missed shots, turnovers, personal fouls), then scaled to a per-minute rate and adjusted for team pace so players on fast teams aren’t automatically inflated by more possessions. Why people call it flawed comes down to what it does and doesn’t encode: 1) It’s box-score only. Anything that doesn’t show up directly—screening, off-ball defense, rim deterrence, positional versatility, scheme fit—won’t be captured unless it indirectly changes rebounds/blocks/steals, etc. 2) Weighting choices embed assumptions. PER assigns specific coefficients to events (e.g., steals and blocks are weighted a lot; missed shots and turnovers penalized; assists rewarded). Those weights aren’t learned from outcomes in a modern predictive sense; they’re mostly heuristic and calibrated to “feel right.” 3) Role and context aren’t handled cleanly. High-usage scorers can score well even if their marginal value is less than a lower-usage player who improves team efficiency. Also, teammates and system affect assist chances, rebound opportunities, etc. 4) Defense is underrepresented and noisy. PER has steals and blocks and defensive rebounds, but team defense is mostly about positioning, contest quality, and preventing shots—none of which are in traditional box score. 5) Normalization makes it “comparable,” but not necessarily in the way people think. It’s normalized to a league average each season, but changes in league style can change what a given PER “means” in terms of winning impact. If you want, I can walk through the actual building blocks (uPER, pace adjustment, league normalization), then contrast it with Win Shares, BPM, and modern plus-minus approaches.
Yes, walk me through the building blocks. Start with uPER and how the weights get applied.

uPER (“unadjusted PER”) is the core per-minute box-score productivity rate before pace and league normalization. The exact published formula is a bit messy because it’s constructed from intermediate terms, but conceptually it’s: - Convert a player’s box-score line into a “net points produced” estimate. - Adjust for team context in a couple places (notably assists and rebounds) using factors like team assist rate and league assist rate. - Divide by minutes to make it per-minute. Key elements and why they exist: 1) Scoring component PER gives credit for made field goals and free throws, and debits for missed shots. But it doesn’t just count points; it tries to value shot attempts and makes in a way that approximates efficiency. - Made FG: positive weight - Missed FG: negative weight - Made FT: positive weight - Missed FT: negative weight (smaller than missed FG) This is meant to reward efficient scoring and penalize inefficient volume. 2) Assists Assists are credited, but the value of an assist is partially “shared” with the teammate who made the shot, and it depends on team context. Hollinger uses an “assist factor” that varies with team assist rate: on teams where most buckets are assisted, an individual assist might be less indicative of special creation; on teams with low assist rates, assists might be more “valuable” as an indicator of playmaking. So uPER includes something like: + (assist coefficient) * AST but that coefficient isn’t constant; it’s scaled by a factor involving team AST/FG and league AST/FG. 3) Rebounds Offensive rebounds are weighted more than defensive rebounds because they extend possessions and often lead to efficient put-backs or reset offense. Defensive boards end possessions but are more common and somewhat more “available” depending on scheme, shot profile, and teammates. Thus: + (higher weight) * ORB + (lower weight) * DRB 4) Defense events: steals and blocks Steals are weighted heavily because they end the opponent’s possession and often create transition opportunities. Blocks are also positive, but blocks can be noisy because not all blocks equal a stop (some go out of bounds, some are recovered by offense). Still, PER values blocks fairly strongly. 5) Turnovers and fouls Turnovers are a big negative because they waste possessions. Personal fouls are also negative (they can lead to free throws and put opponents in the bonus), but in PER they’re a relatively modest penalty compared to turnovers and missed shots. 6) Team adjustment terms There are a couple “team factor” terms that adjust for: - The value of assists given team assisted field goal rate - The allocation between individual and team rebounds (since rebounds are credited individually but are partly a team environment) - Pace adjustment later (not in uPER yet) The reason uPER is complicated is that it tries to avoid double-counting the same value. Example: if a player makes a shot, that’s already credited as scoring; if a teammate assisted, you don’t want to fully credit the assist as if it created points from nothing. So the assist coefficient is tuned to represent marginal value above what’s already credited. A practical way to understand uPER without memorizing the formula: it’s a weighted box-score sum per minute, with strong rewards for efficient scoring, steals, and offensive boards; moderate rewards for assists, blocks, and defensive boards; and penalties for turnovers and missed shots. If you want, I can outline the next steps: pace adjustment to get PER, and then normalization to set league average at 15.
Before pace adjustment, can you give an example with two hypothetical players and show qualitatively why one would have higher uPER?

Sure—qualitatively, because the exact coefficients are finicky, but we can still see how the structure behaves. Player A (high-usage scorer, mediocre efficiency) - 36 minutes - 10/24 FG (2/7 3P, but PER historically didn’t explicitly separate 3P in the earliest versions; later versions do account for 3P makes) - 6/8 FT - 4 AST - 4 ORB, 5 DRB - 1 STL, 0 BLK - 5 TOV - 3 PF Player B (lower-usage, efficient, creates and defends via events) - 36 minutes - 7/11 FG - 2/2 FT - 8 AST - 1 ORB, 6 DRB - 3 STL, 2 BLK - 2 TOV - 2 PF How uPER “likes” these profiles: 1) Shooting efficiency and volume - A has 24 FG attempts for 10 makes: 14 misses. That’s a lot of missed FG negatives. - B has 11 attempts for 7 makes: 4 misses. Much fewer negatives, still decent positives from makes. Even if A scores more raw points, uPER penalizes the inefficiency heavily. 2) Turnovers - A has 5 turnovers: big negative. - B has 2: smaller negative. 3) Assists - B’s 8 assists get a decent positive, scaled by team context. - A’s 4 assists help but not enough to offset higher misses and turnovers. 4) Steals/blocks - B has 3 steals and 2 blocks. PER weights steals strongly; those 3 steals can swing uPER a lot. - A has 1 steal, 0 blocks. 5) Offensive rebounds - A’s 4 ORB are valuable—PER likes ORB. - B only has 1 ORB. So the comparison becomes: A’s extra shot volume and ORB vs B’s efficiency, playmaking, and defensive events with fewer turnovers. In many plausible coefficient sets, Player B ends up with higher uPER because: - Missed shots and turnovers are large negatives. - Steals are large positives. - Efficient scoring is rewarded because you get the make credit without the miss penalties. This illustrates a common PER critique: it can love players who rack up steals/blocks and efficient low-volume scoring, even if their overall defensive impact is more complicated; and it can punish high-usage creators who take difficult shots late in clock, where misses are “necessary” for team offense. If you’d like, I can also show a case where PER overrates someone: e.g., a big who gets lots of defensive rebounds and blocks but doesn’t move the needle on team defense.
Go on. Show that overrated big archetype and explain the mechanism.

The “PER darling big” archetype is typically: - High defensive rebound rate (especially in eras/contexts where bigs vacuum boards) - Decent block rate - Low turnovers (because they don’t handle much) - High FG% on low-difficulty shots (dunks, layups, assisted finishes) - Limited passing/creation responsibility (so few turnovers, few missed self-created shots) - Sometimes mediocre real defensive value (blocks without deterrence, poor positioning, or gives up offensive rebounds/putbacks), but the box score can’t see that. Hypothetical Player C (the archetype) - 28 minutes - 6/8 FG (mostly assisted) - 1/2 FT - 1 AST - 1 ORB, 10 DRB - 1 STL, 3 BLK - 1 TOV - 3 PF Hypothetical Player D (a more valuable but “invisible” defender / connector) - 28 minutes - 4/7 FG - 2/2 FT - 4 AST - 2 ORB, 5 DRB - 1 STL, 1 BLK - 2 TOV - 2 PF Plus: elite screen setting, rotates early, tags rollers, communicates, contests without blocks, boxes out to free teammates for rebounds—none of which is in the box score. Why PER can overrate C relative to D: 1) Defensive rebounds are plentiful and get positive weight. A big with 10 DRB in 28 minutes will rack up a lot of PER value. But DRB are partially “available”: if your team scheme prioritizes having the center drop and rebound while wings leak out, the center gets a huge DRB share. That’s not purely individual impact. 2) Blocks are rewarded, even if they’re “empty.” PER credits blocks as positive defensive events. But a block that goes out of bounds doesn’t end the possession; some blocks are chasedown after a blown assignment; some are gambles that compromise rebounding position. PER doesn’t distinguish. 3) Efficient finishing is rewarded without charging for shot creation. C goes 6/8: lots of make credit, few miss penalties. But if those are mostly spoon-fed, the “value” might belong more to the passer/creator and spacing context. 4) Low turnovers look great. C has 1 turnover because he rarely dribbles or passes into traffic. That’s good, but it also means he’s not asked to make difficult decisions. PER treats low turnovers as purely positive without accounting for responsibility. 5) Passing and connective play is undercounted. D’s 4 assists help, but the coefficient may not fully reflect how valuable it is to keep the offense coherent—especially if those assists are high-leverage (e.g., short-roll reads). Meanwhile, D’s 2 turnovers are penalized. Net effect: C’s line looks like “impact” in PER terms: rebounds + blocks + efficient points + low turnovers. D’s “invisible” defense and screening doesn’t show, so PER doesn’t credit it. This is why analysts often say PER is more a measure of “box-score productivity” than “overall value,” and why it can mis-rank players when roles differ.
Now pace adjustment. How does PER adjust for pace, and why does that matter?

Pace adjustment tries to put players on teams with different possessions per game on a comparable footing. In a faster environment, there are more shot attempts, rebounds, assists, etc. Per-minute stats already partially control for pace, but not fully, because minutes aren’t the same thing as possessions. A team can play fast and spread minutes similarly; players will have more possessions per minute. So Hollinger takes uPER (per-minute box score productivity) and adjusts it by a pace factor roughly based on: - Team pace (possessions per game) - League pace (average possessions per game) The idea is: if you play on a fast team, your per-minute box-score accumulation is inflated because more possessions occur during your minutes. Pace adjustment scales you down. If you play on a slow team, you’re scaled up. Why it matters: - Without pace adjustment, players on high-tempo teams systematically look better because they have more opportunities per minute to generate events. - With pace adjustment, PER is closer to a per-possession productivity rate, though still not perfectly per-possession for the player individually (because it uses team pace, not on-court pace, and not the player’s actual possessions used/defended). There’s also a subtlety: pace adjustment can “over-correct” if a player’s role is tightly linked to pace (e.g., a bench unit that always plays fast regardless of team average) because PER uses team-level pace rather than lineup-level pace. After pace adjustment, the result is PER (before normalization). Then it’s normalized so the league average PER is exactly 15 each season. That normalization is what makes “15 is average” a consistent reference point.
Explain the normalization step. Why set league average to 15, and does it change comparisons across seasons?

Normalization is basically a linear rescaling: take the computed pace-adjusted PER values for all players, compute the league average (weighted by minutes), then multiply everyone by a constant so that the minutes-weighted league average becomes 15. Why 15? - It’s arbitrary but convenient: not too small, not too big, and it gives a familiar “above/below average” scale with room for stars to be in the 20s and elite seasons in the 30s. Does it change comparisons across seasons? - It makes intra-season comparisons straightforward: a 20 PER is “well above average” in that season. - Cross-season comparisons are trickier. Because it forces the average to 15 every year, it removes league-wide shifts in box-score environment from the mean, but not necessarily from the distribution shape or from what “value” a PER point corresponds to in wins. Example: if the league becomes more efficient and turnover rates drop, the mapping from box-score events to wins might shift. PER’s coefficients don’t necessarily re-learn that. Normalization ensures the average stays 15, but the relationship between PER and actual impact (e.g., plus-minus, wins) could change era to era. Also, the pool of minutes matters: if more minutes go to replacement-level players vs concentrated stars, the distribution changes. Normalization doesn’t fix that; it only fixes the mean. So: normalization makes PER a relative, season-specific index. It’s not a universal physics constant.
Great. Now switch to Win Shares. What are Win Shares conceptually, and how are they computed differently from PER?

Win Shares (WS), popularized by Basketball-Reference from Dean Oliver’s framework, aims to allocate team wins to individual players based on their estimated contributions to offense and defense. The core difference from PER: - PER: per-minute box-score productivity index (rate stat), not anchored directly to wins. - WS: a counting stat tied to team wins; it explicitly tries to split wins among players. Conceptually: 1) Estimate team offensive wins produced from points scored and offensive efficiency. 2) Estimate team defensive wins produced from points allowed and defensive efficiency. 3) Allocate those offensive and defensive wins to players based on their individual offensive and defensive “shares” derived from box-score stats. How computed (high-level): - Offensive Win Shares (OWS): derived from Offensive Rating (points produced per 100 possessions) and the player’s minutes/possessions, compared to league average efficiency. It uses box-score inputs to estimate points produced: scoring, assists, offensive rebounds, turnovers, etc. - Defensive Win Shares (DWS): derived from team Defensive Rating (points allowed per 100 possessions) and minutes, then allocated to players using defensive box-score proxies (defensive rebounds, steals, blocks, and especially the fact that it starts from team defense). That last point is crucial: DWS is heavily team-defense anchored. If you play heavy minutes on an elite defensive team, you can accumulate DWS even if your individual defense is not the main driver. Conversely, a great defender on a bad defensive team may get limited DWS. WS vs PER differences: - WS is cumulative: more minutes = more WS, all else equal. - PER is a rate: per-minute; you can have a high PER in limited minutes. - WS is explicitly tied to team wins; PER is not. - WS uses team context more, especially on defense. WS/48 is the rate version (Win Shares per 48 minutes), similar spirit to PER being a rate.
Drill into Offensive Win Shares. How does “points produced” get estimated from box score?

Offensive Win Shares starts with estimating a player’s offensive contribution in terms of points produced and possessions used, then converting that into marginal offense relative to league average. Basketball-Reference’s “Points Produced” is built from: 1) Scoring: field goals and free throws - Credit for made shots and made free throws. - It accounts for the fact that not all made shots are unassisted; some are created by teammates. There’s an assisted/unassisted adjustment so the scorer doesn’t get 100% of the “creation” credit. - It also accounts for 3-point shots explicitly: extra point value. 2) Assists: “Assist points” - Assists are converted into an estimate of points created for teammates. This uses team-level data like team FG% on assisted shots and the distribution of 2s/3s to estimate how many points those assists generated. 3) Offensive rebounds: “ORB points” - ORB are valued because they extend possessions and lead to additional scoring chances; there’s an estimate of how many points an offensive rebound is “worth” based on team points per possession after ORB or league averages. 4) Turnovers: subtract possessions without scoring - Turnovers add to possessions used without producing points, lowering efficiency. Then it estimates “Offensive Rating” (ORtg) for the player: points produced per 100 possessions used. That’s not the same as team ORtg; it’s an individual estimate. Once you have: - Player ORtg - Player possessions used (from FGA, FTA, TOV, and some rebound/assist adjustments) You can compute “marginal offense”: - Compare player ORtg to league average ORtg. - Multiply the difference by possessions to get marginal points. Then marginal points are converted into marginal wins using a “points per win” conversion (related to league scoring environment). Those marginal wins become Offensive Win Shares, scaled so total OWS across team equals team offensive wins. It’s a chain: Box score -> points produced & possessions -> ORtg -> marginal points -> marginal wins -> win shares The key is it’s trying to translate box-score actions into points, then points into wins.
Defensive Win Shares always felt weird. Explain the allocation mechanism and why it’s criticized.

Defensive Win Shares (DWS) starts from team defense, not individual defense. Mechanically: 1) Compute team defensive efficiency (points allowed per 100 possessions), i.e., team Defensive Rating. 2) Convert team defense into “team defensive wins.” - Compare team defensive efficiency to league average. - Translate the difference into marginal points prevented. - Convert points prevented into wins using points-per-win. This yields an estimate of how many wins the team defense produced relative to average. 3) Allocate those team defensive wins to players based on minutes and defensive box-score components. The allocation uses: - Minutes played (big driver) - Defensive rebounds, steals, blocks, personal fouls (and sometimes positional adjustments) But the allocation is constrained so that the sum of players’ DWS equals team defensive wins. Why criticized: - Team effect dominates. If you play big minutes on a great defensive team, you’ll get DWS even if you’re the weak link. - It can punish great defenders on poor defensive teams because there aren’t many “defensive wins” to allocate. - The box-score allocation keys (DRB, STL, BLK) are partial proxies. Great positional defense, switchability, rim deterrence without blocks, and communication aren’t captured. - It can over-credit high-DRB bigs and high-block players because those are prominent in the allocation. So DWS is less “individual defense measurement” and more “share of a team defensive outcome,” with box-score hints about who deserves what share. That’s not useless—minutes on elite defenses are meaningful—but it’s not the same as an individual defensive impact metric.
Compare PER and WS to BPM and VORP. How do BPM/VORP try to fix issues?

BPM (Box Plus/Minus) and VORP (Value Over Replacement Player) are designed to estimate impact in plus-minus terms and value above a baseline, using box score but trained/anchored to plus-minus data. BPM: - Goal: estimate a player’s contribution to point differential per 100 possessions relative to average (plus/minus framework). - Method: regression-style model that uses box-score stats (scoring efficiency, assists, rebounds, steals, blocks, turnovers, etc.) plus position estimates to predict observed plus-minus (often using multi-year data to reduce noise). - Output: BPM (points per 100 possessions above average), split into OBPM and DBPM. Why it’s an improvement over PER/WS in some ways: - Weights are learned to match impact (point differential), not hand-tuned. - It incorporates position/role context more explicitly. - It attempts to allocate defensive impact in a way that correlates with team results, even though it’s still box-score based. Limitations: - Still box-score input, so it can’t see non-box actions directly. - Defensive component (DBPM) is particularly model-dependent and can still be noisy or biased toward certain box-score patterns. - The model can bake in historical relationships that may drift with league style. VORP: - Converts BPM into “value over replacement” by: 1) Defining a replacement-level baseline (e.g., around -2.0 BPM or similar, depending on implementation). 2) Multiplying (BPM - baseline) by minutes/possessions and scaling to a season value. So VORP is cumulative, like WS, but anchored to impact above replacement, like WAR in baseball. Key difference from WS: - WS ties to team wins and uses team defensive performance heavily. - VORP ties to estimated point differential impact and uses a replacement baseline. So BPM/VORP try to fix PER’s “not tied to winning” and WS’s “team-defense allocation” by anchoring to plus-minus concepts, while still being box-score accessible.
Okay, but plus-minus itself is noisy. Can you break down raw plus-minus, on/off, RAPM, and why analysts prefer regularized versions?

Raw plus-minus: - The point differential while a player is on the court. Problem: heavily confounded by teammates, opponents, and lineup context. A player who always plays with starters against bench units will look great. On/off: - (Team net rating with player on) minus (team net rating with player off). Better than raw plus-minus because it uses the player’s own team as a control, but still confounded: - “Off” minutes might be against different opponents. - Rotations matter: if the bench is terrible, a starter’s on/off looks inflated. Adjusted Plus-Minus (APM): - Regression approach: use play-by-play lineup data to estimate each player’s independent effect on point differential, controlling for the other nine players on court. The model tries to solve: given all lineup combinations and outcomes, what player coefficients best explain point differentials? Problem: multicollinearity and noise. - Teammates share many minutes, making it hard to separate their effects. - Sample sizes can be limited, especially for single seasons. - The regression coefficients can swing wildly. Regularized APM (RAPM): - Add regularization (ridge regression / L2 penalty) to shrink coefficients toward zero, reducing variance. This improves out-of-sample stability and makes the estimates more usable, at the cost of some bias (shrinking true extremes). Why analysts prefer regularized versions: - Lower variance: fewer absurd estimates. - Better predictive performance. - More stable year-to-year, especially when combined with priors. From there you get: - Prior-informed variants (e.g., Bayesian RAPM). - Hybrid models (e.g., combining box score and tracking data as priors). So the preference is: raw plus-minus is too context-heavy; APM is too noisy; RAPM is the workable compromise.
How do modern public metrics like EPM, LEBRON, or RAPTOR fit into this taxonomy?

They’re generally “hybrid impact metrics” that combine plus-minus backbone with informative priors from box score and sometimes tracking. Common structure: 1) Start with a regularized plus-minus estimate from play-by-play (RAPM-like). That gives an impact signal but noisy. 2) Use a prior/predictor built from box score (and sometimes player tracking, shot location, lineup roles) to stabilize the estimate, especially early in the season or for low-minute players. 3) Calibrate to an interpretable scale: - Points per 100 possessions (like BPM) - Or per possession / per minute And sometimes convert to wins (WAR-style). Differences among them are mostly in: - What data features they use (box score only vs including shot quality or tracking). - How they model offense vs defense. - How they treat luck factors (3P opponent shooting variance, etc.). - How they handle position, role, and aging curves. RAPTOR (as originally published publicly): - Used box-score components and on/off plus-minus, with priors and adjustments, outputting offense/defense and WAR. LEBRON: - Similar hybrid approach with a plus-minus core and box-score/tracking-informed priors, outputting per-100 impact and WAR. EPM: - Also an impact metric with Bayesian/regularized plus-minus plus priors; tends to emphasize stability and predictive accuracy. The big conceptual shift from PER/WS is: these metrics are explicitly trying to estimate “how many points does this player add/subtract per 100 possessions, controlling for context,” rather than “how many box-score events did they accumulate.”
Bring it back to PER: if PER is flawed, why does it still correlate with “good players”? What’s the signal it’s capturing?

PER captures a real signal because high-level players often do dominate the box score in efficient ways: - They score efficiently and/or at high volume. - They create shots for others (assists). - They rebound (especially bigs). - They generate steals/blocks at above-average rates. - They limit turnovers relative to usage (sometimes). So PER is picking up “box-score dominance per minute.” That correlates with talent and with impact because many forms of value do show up in the box score. The correlation breaks down when: - Defense is mostly positional and not event-driven. - Offensive value comes from spacing gravity, screening, or low-turnover connectivity that doesn’t produce assists. - Role is specialized (3-and-D wings, off-ball movers). - High-usage players take difficult shots that are necessary for the offense, which PER penalizes for misses. But as a rough heuristic, “stuffing the box score efficiently” is usually a good sign, so PER often tracks star-level players, especially ones with ball-in-hand scoring and event defense.
Let’s talk about usage. How does usage rate interact with PER and WS? Does high usage mechanically inflate or deflate them?

Usage rate (USG%) estimates the share of team possessions a player “uses” via FGA, FTA, and turnovers while on the floor. Interaction: PER: - High usage can help if the player maintains efficiency and adds box-score positives (assists, rebounds, steals). - High usage can hurt if it comes with many missed shots and turnovers, because PER penalizes those strongly. - There’s no explicit “usage bonus”; it’s emergent from the weighted events. But practically, players with high usage have more chances to accumulate both positives and negatives. If their efficiency is above average, PER tends to like them. WS: - Since WS is cumulative and tied to marginal points/wins, high usage helps only if it yields above-average efficiency (ORtg above league average). If high usage drags efficiency down, the marginal offense can be small or negative, limiting OWS. - WS also depends on minutes. A moderate-usage player who plays a ton of minutes on a great team can rack up WS. So high usage doesn’t mechanically inflate either; it increases variance. It can create high PER/OWS if paired with efficiency and low turnovers; it can crater them if inefficient. One subtlety: PER’s per-minute nature can make high-usage bench scorers in limited minutes look very good if they score efficiently against second units. WS, being tied to total wins and minutes, may not elevate them as much unless they play big minutes.
Can you explain why some analysts dislike PER specifically for ranking defenders and wings?

For defenders and wings, the main issues are: 1) Defensive value is not event-only. Many elite wing defenders: - Force tough shots without blocks. - Navigate actions to prevent advantages. - Execute rotations that prevent open threes. Those don’t show as steals/blocks, so PER misses them. 2) Rebounding bias by position. Bigs naturally get more rebounds; wings’ defensive impact is often not rebounding-based. PER weights rebounds positively, so bigs get a structural advantage. 3) Assist bias toward on-ball roles. Many wings provide value as secondary attackers, quick decisions, hockey assists, spacing, and off-ball movement. PER credits assists but not “advantage creation” that doesn’t end in an assist. 4) 3-and-D archetype undervaluation. A wing who shoots spot-up threes, defends the best scorer, and rarely turns it over might have: - Moderate points - Low assists - Low rebounds - Low steals/blocks PER may see them as mediocre even if lineup impact is strong. 5) Personal fouls and matchup difficulty not captured well. A wing taking the hardest defensive assignment might foul more or have fewer steals; PER penalizes fouls modestly but doesn’t credit assignment difficulty. So PER can underrate “glue” wings and overrate certain event-heavy players, especially bigs.

Now I want a concrete mini-case study. Take two player profiles: an on-ball heliocentric guard and an off-ball wing stopper. Describe how PER, WS, BPM, and RAPM-type metrics might rank them differently and why.

Heliocentric guard (Player G): - 35 minutes - USG% 33 - 28 points on 59% TS - 9 assists, 4 turnovers - 4 rebounds - Low steals/blocks - Defensive reputation mixed Team offense collapses without him; he creates most advantages. Wing stopper (Player W): - 34 minutes - USG% 16 - 14 points on 61% TS (mostly spot-ups/cuts) - 2 assists, 1 turnover - 5 rebounds - 1.5 steals, 0.7 blocks - Takes toughest perimeter matchup; suppresses opponent efficiency; high matchup difficulty. How metrics might treat them: PER: - Likely favors G strongly because of scoring volume + assists, provided efficiency is good and turnovers not too high. Even with average defense, the offensive box score dominates. - W may look only slightly above average: efficient but low volume, modest assists. If steals are decent, that helps, but lack of big box-score counting stats can keep PER modest. WS: - OWS: G likely strong because he produces lots of points and assists at above-average efficiency on high possessions. - DWS: depends heavily on team defense. If W is on a good defensive team, he racks DWS; if the team defense is mediocre, his DWS might not pop despite real impact. Overall WS likely still favors G if he’s the offensive engine on a winning team, but W can close the gap if team defense is elite and W plays heavy minutes. BPM: - OBPM: G high because box score screams creation. BPM’s learned weights usually love high-usage efficient scoring + assists. - DBPM: W might get some credit via steals/blocks/rebounds, but DBPM is still box-score based and may not fully capture “stopper” value. G’s DBPM might be negative or near neutral depending on steals, rebounds, position estimate. Overall BPM likely favors G, but W could look better than in PER if BPM values low turnovers and efficient shooting and gives some defensive credit. RAPM / hybrid impact: - Here W has a chance to shine if his defense truly swings point differential and the data captures it over enough minutes and lineup contexts. - G will also grade well if the offense is dramatically better with him and the model can separate him from teammates. The ranking could be closer than PER suggests, or even flip in some contexts (e.g., if G’s bench lineups struggle defensively and W anchors elite defensive units). Hybrid metrics might show G as +X on offense, W as +Y on defense, with net impacts comparable. This is the core idea: box-score-only metrics tend to privilege on-ball creation; impact metrics can elevate off-ball defense if it shows up in lineup results.
You mentioned TS%. How do shooting efficiency measures (TS%, eFG%) relate to these metrics? Do PER and WS properly account for the value of threes?

TS% (True Shooting %) measures scoring efficiency including threes and free throws: TS% = Points / (2 * (FGA + 0.44*FTA)) eFG% adjusts FG% for the extra point of threes: eFG% = (FGM + 0.5*3PM) / FGA How they relate: PER: - Modern PER versions incorporate 3P makes explicitly (earlier iterations were less precise). It rewards made threes because they’re made field goals and yield more points; but the exact weighting is baked into the formula rather than explicitly using TS%. - It does penalize missed shots; so efficient shooters benefit. - It can still misvalue shot types because it doesn’t explicitly model shot value per attempt the way TS% does; it approximates via weights. WS: - Offensive Rating / points produced explicitly accounts for 3P and FT and uses estimated points produced, so it does capture threes’ extra value. - Because it computes points produced and possessions, it aligns more naturally with TS%-like efficiency. BPM: - Uses box-score efficiency components; it generally tracks TS% and 3P value reasonably because it’s trained to plus-minus outcomes in eras where threes matter. So: WS and BPM tend to incorporate three-point value more structurally. PER does too in later versions, but because it’s a fixed-weight index, it can lag behind changes in shot profile value across eras (e.g., corner threes, spacing effects) unless recalibrated.
Let’s talk about “gravity” and spacing. Box score doesn’t see it. How do impact metrics pick it up, and how can we infer it without tracking data?

Gravity/spacing is the value a shooter provides by forcing defensive attention, which can improve teammates’ efficiency. Box score misses it because it doesn’t show as an assist, rebound, etc. Impact metrics pick it up indirectly: - If lineups with the shooter consistently produce better offense (higher points per possession) even when the shooter isn’t taking many shots, plus-minus models attribute some portion of that improvement to the shooter, after controlling for teammates/opponents. - RAPM-like methods can capture it if the shooter’s minutes are varied enough across different lineups to identify their effect. Without tracking data, inference approaches include: 1) On/off splits with controls: - Compare team offensive rating with shooter on vs off, but you must adjust for teammate/opponent quality (e.g., using regression, or looking at “with/without” for key teammates). - If multiple lineups improve in similar ways when the shooter plays, that’s suggestive. 2) Teammate shot profile changes: - Look at how teammates’ rim frequency, corner three frequency, and TS% change when the shooter is on the floor. If teammates get more efficient looks, it’s consistent with spacing impact. 3) Assisted/unassisted changes: - If teammates’ assisted rates rise (more catch-and-shoot) with the shooter, it can indicate improved shot quality due to defensive attention. 4) Lineup archetype comparisons: - Compare lineups identical except swapping the shooter for a non-shooter in similar role. If the offense improves materially, that gap is likely spacing. Caveat: all these are vulnerable to confounding and selection (coaches may play the shooter with better creators). That’s why plus-minus regression is preferred: it tries to deconfound.
Another critique: PER overvalues steals. Is that fair? What’s the actual value of a steal in points, roughly?

It’s fair in the sense that PER gives steals a large coefficient relative to many other events. Whether that’s “overvalued” depends on what you think the average steal is worth. A steal typically: - Ends an opponent possession (preventing expected points on that possession). - Often creates a transition opportunity (increasing your expected points on the next possession). If league average points per possession (PPP) is ~1.10–1.15 in modern eras, then: - Ending a possession prevents ~1.10 expected points (roughly). - The ensuing possession might be slightly above average if it’s transition; say +0.05 to +0.15 PPP relative to baseline, depending on era and how often it becomes a true fast opportunity. So a steal could be worth on the order of ~1.2 to ~1.4 points of net swing in expectation, maybe more in some contexts. But: - Not all steals are equal; some are gambles that increase opponent shot quality when they fail. - High-steal players may take risks that harm team defense in other ways. - Steal rate can be partially a function of scheme. PER can’t see the “failed gambles,” so it may treat steals as pure value. That’s the critique: it rewards the visible positive without subtracting the invisible cost. Impact metrics can, in theory, net this out: if a player gets steals but compromises defense, their net defensive impact may not be great.
How does PER treat offensive rebounds versus defensive rebounds? You said ORB is weighted more. Why, and is that justified?

ORB are weighted more because they extend a possession that was about to end. The marginal value of an offensive rebound is high because it creates an extra scoring chance without the defense being fully set (often). Empirically, possessions after offensive rebounds tend to be reasonably efficient, though not as efficient as pure transition; still, they’re valuable. Defensive rebounds: - End the opponent’s possession, which is good, but defensive rebounds are more “shared” and often a function of team scheme and shot profile. - Many defensive rebounds are uncontested; the marginal value of one additional defensive rebound for a particular player is smaller because someone on the team was likely to get it anyway. So weighting ORB more is conceptually justified. Where it can go wrong: - Some ORB come at a cost: if a player crashes the glass aggressively, it might increase opponent transition opportunities. Box score credits the ORB but doesn’t debit the transition concessions. - Team context matters: if the scheme emphasizes sending players back, ORB opportunities differ. Still, as a general rule, ORB are more valuable than DRB on a per-event basis, so the directional weighting makes sense.
WS also values ORB via possessions and points produced, right? How do their treatments differ?

Yes, both value ORB, but differently: PER: - Directly adds a positive coefficient for ORB and a smaller one for DRB. It’s a linear event weight approach. WS (via points produced / ORtg): - ORB increase a player’s estimated possessions “extended” and contribute to points produced. The value of an ORB is tied to the expected points on the resulting extended possession, which is more context-sensitive. - ORB also interacts with possessions used and efficiency. A player can add ORB but if they also have inefficient put-backs, their ORtg might not rise much. So WS can, in theory, tie ORB to actual scoring value more explicitly. PER just “pays” for ORB as a good thing. In practice, both like ORB-heavy players, but WS’s chain to points and wins can temper it if the subsequent scoring isn’t efficient.
I want to understand “replacement level” baselines. WS doesn’t use replacement level, but VORP does. How do we define replacement level in basketball metrics, and what are the pitfalls?

Replacement level is the expected performance of a readily available player—someone you can sign/call up to fill minutes at low cost. In metrics, it’s used to convert “above average” into “above replacement,” which is more aligned with roster-building value. Definitions vary: - Some set replacement as a fixed BPM baseline (e.g., around -2.0 per 100 possessions). - Some use a percentile of player performance distribution weighted by minutes. - Some tie it to empirical performance of minimum-salary/bench players. Pitfalls: 1) It’s not directly observable. There’s no single pool of “replacement players” because teams choose who plays; the observed bottom of the league includes development projects, specialists, and injured-return players, not a pure replacement sample. 2) Position and role matter. Replacement for a center isn’t identical to replacement for a point guard; scarcity differs. 3) Minutes distribution bias. If replacement is defined from players who actually get minutes, you exclude true fringe players who don’t play, biasing replacement upward. 4) Era and league depth changes. Expansion, international talent flow, and development systems change the depth of the league, shifting replacement. Despite that, replacement level is useful because it aligns value with “how many wins above a cheap alternative” a player provides. It’s a roster construction lens rather than pure descriptive performance.
Let’s do a technical detour: what’s the relationship between points per win and Pythagorean expectation? How do WS-like systems convert points into wins?

Points per win is a conversion factor: how many net points (point differential) correspond to one win over a season. Pythagorean expectation relates win percentage to points scored and allowed: Win% ≈ (PF^x) / (PF^x + PA^x) where x is an exponent (often around 13–16 in basketball depending on era). For small changes, you can approximate marginal wins from marginal point differential using derivatives of the Pythagorean formula around a team’s current PF/PA (or around league average). That yields an estimate like: Marginal wins ≈ (marginal points) / (points per win) Points per win depends on scoring environment and variance; in the NBA it’s often around ~30 points per win in modern scoring environments, but it varies. WS-like systems: - Estimate marginal points (offense created above average + defense prevented above average). - Divide by points per win to get marginal wins. - Then scale/allocate to players. The pitfall is that points per win is not constant across team quality: converting points to wins is nonlinear (diminishing returns when you’re already very good), and close-game luck can matter. Many systems just use a league-average conversion for simplicity.
How should we think about “descriptive” vs “predictive” metrics? PER seems descriptive. Are impact metrics predictive?

Descriptive metrics: explain what happened. - PER: describes box-score productivity in a season. - WS: describes win allocation based on team results and box score. Predictive metrics: aim to forecast future performance or future impact. - Some impact metrics (EPM, etc.) are tuned for predictive validity, using priors and stabilization. But impact metrics can be both descriptive and predictive depending on construction: - A pure RAPM over a season is descriptive of that season’s lineup outcomes, but noisy; it might not predict next season well without priors. - A “predictive RAPM” uses multi-year data and shrinkage to improve forecasting. So the spectrum: - Highly descriptive: single-season raw plus-minus, single-season PER. - Mixed: WS, BPM (descriptive but somewhat stable). - More predictive: multi-year RAPM with priors; hybrid metrics with aging curves. Analysts choose based on use case: - Awards discussions: descriptive. - Team decision-making: predictive.
If I’m evaluating a player for “how good they were last season,” what’s a sensible workflow using multiple metrics without being fooled?

A reasonable workflow: 1) Start with role and minutes: - Minutes played matter; value is constrained by availability. - Identify role: primary creator, secondary, big, wing stopper, etc. 2) Use a box-score rate metric for quick scan: - PER or BPM (BPM preferable) to see box-score dominance. - Look at TS%, usage, assist rate, turnover rate for context. 3) Use a value/cumulative metric: - WS and/or VORP to incorporate minutes and tie to team outcomes or replacement. 4) Use at least one impact metric: - RAPM-type or a public hybrid (EPM/LEBRON/RAPTOR) for context-controlled impact. - Check on/off splits and lineup performance to see if the signal is consistent. 5) Sanity-check with team context: - If a player’s DWS is huge but team defense is elite and they share minutes with other elite defenders, be cautious. - If a player’s impact metric is huge but minutes are low or lineup context is narrow, be cautious. 6) Look for convergence and understand divergence: - If box score and impact agree: confidence increases. - If they diverge: ask “what value is missing from box score?” or “is plus-minus noisy due to lineup confounding?” This reduces the risk of overreacting to one metric’s bias.
I want to go deeper on BPM. How does it incorporate position, and why does position matter for interpreting box-score stats?

Position matters because the same box-score stat can imply different things depending on role. Example: - 8 rebounds from a guard is more “unusual” and may correlate with defensive impact or transition starts. - 8 rebounds from a center is normal. BPM handles this by estimating a player’s position (or role) from their box-score profile (e.g., assist rate, rebound rate, height proxies in some versions) and then applying position adjustments in the regression model. Mechanically (conceptually): - The model includes interactions between stats and position. For instance, assists for a big might be weighted differently than assists for a guard because it signals different offensive creation types. - It also includes a baseline positional value: certain positions historically correlate with certain impacts given the same box score. Why it matters: - Without position context, bigs would dominate because they get more rebounds and blocks. - Position adjustment tries to prevent systematic bias in favor of bigs and against wings/guards. But it’s imperfect: - Modern “positionless” roles blur categories. - A player’s role can shift depending on lineup, and box-score-derived position can misclassify. Still, it’s a major reason BPM often “feels” more reasonable than PER in player rankings.
How do you reconcile when BPM loves a player but RAPM doesn’t, or vice versa?

Interpret it as “different lenses” and look for the reason: Case 1: BPM high, RAPM low Possible explanations: - Box-score production doesn’t translate to lineup results (empty calories). - Defensive shortcomings not captured by box score. - High-usage scoring that’s efficient individually but doesn’t improve team offense due to play style or fit. - RAPM sample noise or tough lineup context (always with weak lineups or against strong opponents). What to check: - Team offensive rating with the player on vs off. - Shot profile changes for teammates. - Turnover and foul context. - Opponent quality in minutes (if available). Case 2: RAPM high, BPM low Possible explanations: - Player provides spacing, screening, or defensive positioning not in box score. - Player fits lineups and elevates others. - BPM undervalues low-usage connectors. - RAPM could be inflated by always playing with stars (though adjusted models try to control for that). What to check: - With/without data for key teammates: does the player help across different combinations? - Does the team defense improve with the player even without steals/blocks? - Stability across seasons. In general, repeated multi-season patterns are more trustworthy than one-season divergences.
Let’s analyze a common argument: “PER rewards volume scoring too much.” Is that true, given it penalizes misses? How would you test it?

It’s partially true but nuanced. PER rewards scoring because points are a big part of the weighted sum. It does penalize misses, so inefficient volume is punished. But if a player has: - High volume - Slightly above-average efficiency They can get a very high PER because the makes and free throws accumulate and the miss penalties aren’t enough to offset the sheer volume. How to test empirically: 1) Correlate PER with volume stats: - PER vs points per game, PER vs usage. - Control for TS% (partial correlation). If PER still correlates strongly with usage after controlling for efficiency, that suggests volume is overweighted. 2) Compare PER to impact metrics: - Identify players with high PER but modest impact (RAPM/EPM). - See if they share traits: high usage, moderate efficiency, weak defense. 3) Sensitivity analysis: - Use a simple linear model: PER ~ a*(TS% relative to league) + b*(usage) + c*(AST%) + d*(REB%) + e*(STL/BLK) + ... Estimate coefficients; if b is large, that indicates usage drives PER beyond efficiency. 4) Construct “counterfactuals”: - Take a player’s line and scale down shot attempts while keeping efficiency constant; see how PER changes.
