If PER drops a lot, it’s volume-sensitive. In practice, PER is quite scoring-centric, and the “too much” depends on your value framework. If you believe primary creation is the hardest and most valuable skill, you might accept a scoring-heavy metric; if you want all-around impact, you’ll see it as skewed.
Another argument: WS is “team wins” disguised as individual value. Is that fair?

Partly fair. WS is constrained by team wins: - Total Win Shares on a team roughly sum to team wins (with some scaling conventions). So players on winning teams have more WS to “share.” That makes WS partly a function of team quality and minutes distribution. However, WS still tries to allocate based on individual contributions: - OWS depends on individual efficiency and possessions used. - DWS depends on minutes and defensive box score, but anchored to team defense. So WS is not purely “team wins,” but it is heavily team-contextual. The fair critique is: - It’s a win allocation system, not a pure measurement of individual impact independent of team. This can be a feature if you want a stat that “adds up” to wins, but it’s a bug if you want to compare players across very different team contexts.
How do analysts evaluate defense with limited data? If we avoid tracking and only use public stats, what’s the best we can do?

With limited public data, the best approach is triangulation: 1) Use impact metrics with a defensive component: - DBPM, defensive RAPM/hybrid defensive impact (if available). Even if imperfect, they incorporate lineup results. 2) Use team defense on/off: - Team defensive rating with player on vs off. Adjust mentally for lineup context (who else is on the floor). 3) Use opponent shooting indicators carefully: - Opponent FG% at rim when player is on (if available publicly) can proxy rim protection, but it’s noisy and role-dependent. 4) Use event rates with caution: - Steal rate and block rate are informative but incomplete. - Defensive rebound rate can indicate finishing possessions, but also role/scheme. 5) Foul rate: - High foul rate can indicate poor positioning or high-risk defense, but also role (rim protectors contest more). 6) Multi-year stability: - Defensive metrics are noisier; look for consistent signal across seasons rather than one-year spikes. Without tracking, you can’t fully measure “containment,” “screen navigation,” “help timing,” etc. So you accept uncertainty and rely more on impact + multi-year patterns.
Let’s go even more technical: can you explain why collinearity is so severe in APM/RAPM and how ridge regression helps mathematically?

In APM, you model lineup outcomes as: y = Xβ + ε where: - y is the vector of observed point differentials per stint (or per possession chunk), - X is a matrix indicating which players are on court (e.g., +1 for home players, -1 for away players), - β are player impact coefficients. Collinearity happens because many players share minutes together: - If two players are almost always on the court together, their columns in X are highly correlated. - The regression can’t distinguish which one is responsible for the observed outcomes, so the solution has high variance: small changes in data lead to big swings in β. Ordinary least squares (OLS) tries to minimize squared error, but when X'X is nearly singular (ill-conditioned), the inverse (X'X)^(-1) blows up, leading to unstable estimates. Ridge regression adds an L2 penalty: min ||y - Xβ||^2 + λ||β||^2 Solution: β_ridge = (X'X + λI)^(-1) X'y Adding λI makes the matrix invertible and better conditioned, shrinking coefficients toward zero. This reduces variance and improves stability, especially for players with limited independent lineup variation. Interpretation: - You accept some bias (true impacts are shrunk) to get much lower variance (less noise). - The choice of λ controls the bias-variance tradeoff. That’s why regularization is essential for usable plus-minus estimates.
Nice. Now, can we connect PER/WS to these regression ideas? Are PER and WS implicitly assuming a linear model too?

They’re not regression models, but they do embed linear valuation assumptions. PER: - Explicitly linear in box-score events: PER ≈ Σ (weight_i * stat_i) per minute, plus adjustments. That’s essentially a linear model with hand-chosen coefficients, not fit to outcomes. WS: - More nonlinear because it goes through possessions and efficiency calculations, but many subcomponents are linear allocations: - Points produced components are linear-ish in makes, assists, etc. - Defensive shares are allocated via linear weights and minutes. Then it maps marginal points to wins via a conversion factor (which is approximately linear locally). So yes, conceptually they assume additive separability: each event adds/subtracts value independently. Real basketball value has interactions (spacing changes shot quality, defensive scheme dependencies), which linear box-score models can’t capture well. Regression-based impact metrics try to learn coefficients from outcomes and implicitly capture some interactions through lineup combinations, but even they are linear in player effects (additive β terms), which is another simplifying assumption.
What about “luck” components? For example, opponent 3P% variance affecting defensive metrics. How do impact models account for that?

This is a big issue for defense. Opponent 3P% allowed is noisy: - Defense influences shot quality and contest, but the make/miss variance is high. - Over a season, a team’s opponent 3P% can swing due to randomness. Impact models handle this in a few ways: 1) Regularization and priors: - Shrink defensive estimates toward zero to avoid overreacting to noisy outcomes. 2) Multi-year samples: - Use multiple seasons of data to reduce variance. 3) Separating shot quality from results (if tracking/shot data): - Some models use expected points based on shot location and contest rather than actual makes, which reduces luck. 4) Incorporate luck adjustments: - Some public metrics regress opponent shooting to league average in defensive components. Without shot-quality data, you can’t fully remove luck, but you can reduce its effect by using larger samples and shrinkage. This is also why single-season defensive RAPM can be volatile.
Let’s do a thought experiment: if we redesigned PER today, what would you change while keeping it box-score-only?

If constrained to box-score-only, improvements could be: 1) Re-fit weights to outcomes: - Use regression to fit coefficients that best predict team point differential or wins from player box-score rates, controlling for minutes and position. That turns PER from heuristic to learned. 2) Better possession framing: - Make it per-possession rather than per-minute, using player possessions used and estimated defensive possessions faced. 3) Explicit three-point and free-throw modeling: - Ensure shot value is captured correctly, including 3PA, 3PM, FT rate, and efficiency. 4) Reduce over-reward for defensive events: - Keep steals/blocks valuable but calibrate to their observed marginal impact, net of correlation with gambling. 5) Include interaction proxies: - Even without tracking, you can include team-level spacing proxies or lineup shooting context, but that stretches “box-score-only.” 6) Add role adjustment: - Use usage and assist rate to classify roles and apply different baselines. At that point, you’re basically reinventing BPM. Which is why many people say BPM is the “modern PER” in spirit: box-score-based but outcome-anchored.
Let’s compare PER and BPM directly on interpretability. PER is normalized to 15; BPM is points per 100 possessions. Which is better and why?

BPM’s scale (points per 100 possessions above average) is more directly interpretable because it maps to scoreboard impact. If a player is +5 BPM, the model says they add about 5 points per 100 possessions versus average, holding context constant (in the model’s approximation). You can translate that into wins with minutes and points-per-win. PER’s scale is arbitrary: - 15 average, 20 good, 25 star, 30 MVP-ish, but the “distance” between 18 and 22 isn’t directly “points” or “wins.” So for interpretability: - BPM is better for connecting to team performance. - PER is simpler for casual ranking within a season. But BPM’s interpretability can be overstated because it’s still a model estimate, and the defensive split can be shaky. Still, the unit is meaningful.
Is WS/48 comparable to BPM in interpretability?

WS/48 is “win shares per 48 minutes,” so it’s a rate of wins allocated per full game of playing time. League average WS/48 is around 0.100 (by construction-ish, because total WS equals team wins and minutes distribution sets the average). So: - 0.100 is average, - 0.150 is very good, - 0.200+ is elite. Interpretability: - It’s closer to wins, which is nice. - But because WS is an allocation system tied to team wins, WS/48 is less “independent impact” than BPM. - Also, WS/48 can be inflated for players on strong teams due to defensive anchoring and team context. So WS/48 is interpretable as “share of wins per minute,” but not as a clean causal impact on wins.
How do these metrics handle garbage time? Does it matter?

It can matter, especially for bench players. Box-score metrics (PER, WS, BPM): - Typically include all minutes unless explicitly filtered. - Garbage time can inflate efficiency (against weaker lineups) or deflate (randomness), affecting rate stats like PER and BPM. Plus-minus metrics: - Some implementations filter garbage time because lineup data in non-competitive minutes is less informative about true ability and more about end-of-bench matchups. - If not filtered, a player who dominates garbage time could look better. In practice: - For stars, garbage time is a small share of minutes. - For deep bench players, it can be a big share and can distort evaluation. Analysts often prefer metrics that either filter garbage time or use priors that reduce the influence of low-leverage minutes.
I’ve heard PER is “pace adjusted” but not “league strength adjusted.” Is that even a concept in a single league?

Within one league (NBA), “league strength” is mostly constant within a season, but across eras it changes: rule changes, talent distribution, expansion, style. PER normalization to 15 each season makes it relative to that season’s league average. That implicitly adjusts for league environment in the mean, but not necessarily for: - Distribution of talent (how top-heavy the league is) - Changes in the relationship between box-score events and winning - Changes in positional roles (more threes, fewer post-ups) So “league strength adjusted” could mean: - A way to compare a 1990 season to a 2025 season on a common value scale. PER is not designed for that. It’s designed for within-season comparisons. Cross-era comparisons are inherently hard; you’d need an era translation model that accounts for pace, efficiency, and how stats map to wins in each era.

Suppose I just want to answer the original question to a friend: “PER measures box score per-minute productivity but misses defense and context.” Can you craft a crisp explanation plus a couple examples of where it misleads?

PER is a pace-adjusted, per-minute index built from weighted box-score stats. It rewards efficient scoring, rebounds, assists, steals, and blocks, and penalizes missed shots and turnovers. It’s normalized so league average is 15. Why it can mislead: - It can overrate bigs who get lots of rebounds/blocks and finish easy shots but don’t actually drive team defense. Example: a center with 10 defensive rebounds and 3 blocks in 28 minutes can post a huge PER even if opponents still score efficiently because the blocks/rebounds look great in the box score. - It can underrate wings who defend and space the floor without big counting stats. Example: a low-usage wing who hits threes, rarely turns it over, and defends the best scorer might have a modest PER because steals/blocks/rebounds/assists aren’t eye-popping, even if the team plays much better with him on the court. That’s the clean framing: PER is good for “who filled the box score efficiently,” not perfect for “who helped win the most.”
Good. Now keep going: I want to understand the philosophical difference between “value” and “talent” in analytics terms. How do PER and WS relate to those concepts?

“Talent” is an underlying ability level—what a player would contribute in a neutral context, often framed as a true-skill parameter. “Value” is realized contribution in a specific season: minutes played, role, team context, health/availability, and leverage all matter. PER: - Closer to a descriptive “production rate” than talent or value. It measures what happened in box score per minute, which can reflect talent but also role and context. - It can be used as a rough talent proxy if you average over multiple seasons and adjust for role changes, but it’s not designed for that. WS: - Closer to “value” because it’s cumulative and tied to team wins. If you play more minutes on a winning team and have good efficiency, you accumulate WS. - But it mixes individual contribution with team outcomes, which can blur talent vs context. Impact metrics with priors: - Often explicitly try to estimate talent (true impact) by shrinking noisy season outcomes toward a prior based on history, age, and box score. So: PER is a production index; WS is value allocation; neither is a pure “talent” estimate, though both correlate with it.
How do you build priors for player impact? What goes into them?

Priors can be built from: 1) Multi-year performance: - Past impact metrics (RAPM, BPM, etc.) provide a baseline. 2) Box-score indicators: - Efficiency (TS% relative to league) - Usage and creation (assist rate, turnover rate) - Rebounding and defensive events These are more stable than single-season plus-minus for some players. 3) Age curves: - Players tend to improve early, peak, then decline. Priors incorporate expected aging. 4) Position/role archetypes: - Different archetypes have different stability (e.g., shooters vs bigs reliant on athleticism; though we’re avoiding physical framing, think “skill profile stability”). 5) Minutes and uncertainty: - Low-minute players get stronger shrinkage (more prior weight). - High-minute players get more data weight. Mathematically, a Bayesian framework: Posterior impact ≈ (data precision * observed impact + prior precision * prior mean) / (data precision + prior precision) This yields stable estimates and better predictions.
Let’s revisit WS defense. If DWS is team-anchored, is there a better purely box-score defensive value metric?

Purely box-score defense is inherently limited, but you can do better than DWS by: 1) Event-based defensive estimates: - Use steals, blocks, defensive rebounds, and fouls with weights learned from how they correlate with team defensive efficiency or point differential, controlling for position. This is similar to DBPM’s approach. 2) Position-adjusted defensive box score: - Compare a player’s defensive events to typical rates for their role/position. 3) Incorporate opponent turnover rate and foul drawing indirectly: - But without play-by-play, it’s hard. Still, any purely box-score defensive metric will miss: - Contest quality - Scheme execution - Communication and rotations - Deterrence So the “best” tends to be: use box-score defense as a weak prior, then let impact data do the heavy lifting.
How do you interpret a player with high WS but mediocre WS/48?

That means: - They accumulated a lot of total WS mainly through minutes played (availability and role). - But their per-minute win allocation is only average-ish. Interpretation: - They were valuable in a “floor-raising through minutes” sense: a solid contributor who played a lot. - They may not have been highly efficient or impactful per possession. This is common for durable starters on good teams: not stars, but they play 2,500+ minutes and end up with high total WS.
Conversely, high WS/48 but low total WS?

That’s usually: - Very efficient or impactful per minute, but limited minutes. Reasons could be: - Bench role - Limited season minutes (rest, rotation) - Developmental usage It suggests strong per-minute contribution, but total season value is capped by playing time. It’s a “rate vs volume” distinction, like PER vs total points.
How do analysts handle small sample sizes early in the season? PER can spike. What’s the best practice?

Early season: - Treat rate stats as highly volatile. Best practice: 1) Use priors: regress toward multi-year averages or career norms. 2) Use stabilizing components: - Shooting percentage is noisy; shot volume and free throw rate stabilize faster. - Turnover rate stabilizes moderately. 3) Look at process stats: - Attempt rates (3PA rate, rim attempt rate) - Usage and role 4) For impact metrics, use prior-weighted versions (hybrids) rather than raw RAPM. So if someone has a 28 PER after 10 games, you ask: - Did role change (usage, minutes)? - Is efficiency plausible or driven by hot shooting? - Do impact metrics and lineup performance agree?
Can you explain the concept of “stabilization” of stats? Which stats stabilize fastest?

Stabilization refers to how quickly a stat becomes a reliable indicator of true talent as sample size increases. In general: - Rates based on frequent events stabilize faster. - Percentages based on fewer attempts stabilize slower. In basketball: - Free throw percentage stabilizes relatively slowly because attempts may be low for many players. - Three-point percentage stabilizes slowly for low-volume shooters; faster for high-volume. - Turnover rate can stabilize faster because turnovers happen relatively frequently for ball-handlers. - Rebound rate stabilizes relatively quickly for bigs with lots of opportunities. - Steal/block rates can be noisy but can stabilize moderately with enough minutes. Analysts often use Bayesian shrinkage: treat observed rate as a mix of player true rate and league average, weighted by attempts.
I’m curious: how do you convert between a box-score metric and expected wins? Like, can we say “1 PER point equals X wins”?

Not reliably in a universal way. PER is a per-minute index normalized to 15, not calibrated to wins. You can empirically fit a relationship: - Team-level: sum of player minutes-weighted PER correlates with team wins. - Player-level: PER correlates with impact and value. But the mapping “1 PER point = X wins” varies by: - Minutes played - Position/role - Era - Team context If you really wanted, you could: 1) Compute “PER above average” = PER - 15. 2) Multiply by minutes to get a “PER points” total. 3) Regress team wins on team totals, controlling for other factors. That gives an average conversion, but it’s a rough proxy and not stable. Metrics designed for wins (WS, VORP-to-wins conversions, WAR) are better for that.
Okay. Now, critique Win Shares in the modern era of threes and switching. Does the model’s structure age poorly?

Some aspects age fine (points and possessions logic), but some can be stressed: 1) Defensive allocation: - Switching and perimeter-oriented defense makes individual defensive value less tied to blocks/rebounds. - DWS allocation still leans on those box-score proxies and team defense, so it may misattribute value. 2) Offensive creation vs spacing: - Points produced models can undervalue spacing that changes teammate shot quality without showing as assists. - In a three-heavy era, gravity and off-ball value matter more. 3) Role specialization: - Modern offenses use specialists (shooters, short-roll passers) whose value may not be captured fully by traditional points produced formulas. 4) Team context dependence: - As lineups become more dynamic, team defensive rating might be less informative about any single player’s defense. Still, WS remains a decent descriptive stat for “who played big minutes on good teams and produced efficiently,” but it’s not the final word.
I want a deeper dive into “points produced” and why it’s hard. For example, how do you avoid double-counting points between the scorer and assister?

Double-counting is a core challenge. If a player scores 2 points on a made shot, those 2 points “happened.” If you also credit the assister with “creating” 2 points, you’d be attributing 4 points of value to a 2-point outcome. So points-produced frameworks do something like: - Allocate most of the points to the scorer (because finishing matters). - Allocate some fraction to the assister based on the idea that the assist contributed to shot quality and creation. - The fraction depends on context (team assisted rate, shot type mix). Similarly, offensive rebounds: - If you credit an ORB as worth X points, but the subsequent put-back is also credited as points scored, you might double-count unless you treat ORB as creating extra possessions rather than points directly. Dean Oliver-style frameworks manage this by: - Tracking possessions and points separately. - Assigning credit for extending possessions (ORB) and creating shots (AST) in a way that sums to team points produced. But because we don’t observe actual “creation value” directly, these allocations are model assumptions. That’s why different systems can disagree.
How does this compare to baseball, where linear weights are common? Is basketball just harder because of interactions?

Basketball is harder for a few reasons: 1) Interdependence on every possession: - Every basket involves multiple players: screens, spacing, passes, gravity, etc. - Box score captures only a subset (scorer, assister sometimes). 2) Fluid roles: - Players’ roles change by lineup and matchup; baseball roles are more discrete. 3) Defense is collective: - One defender’s positioning affects another’s responsibilities; credit assignment is complex. 4) Sample size and event frequency: - A basketball season has far fewer discrete “plate appearances” equivalents. Possessions are many, but individual isolated events are fewer and noisier. Linear weights work well when events are relatively separable and outcomes can be attributed cleanly. Basketball has strong interaction effects, making pure linear weights (like PER) less satisfying. That’s why lineup-based methods (plus-minus) are more attractive: they naturally incorporate interactions, though they bring their own noise and identification problems.
Let’s do an argument map: when is PER useful, when is WS useful, and when are they actively misleading?

PER useful: - Quick snapshot of box-score productivity per minute. - Comparing similar-role players within a season (two scoring guards, two rim-running bigs). - Identifying bench players with strong per-minute production. PER misleading: - Comparing across roles/positions without context (bigs vs wings). - Evaluating defense-heavy players whose value isn’t steals/blocks. - Evaluating high-usage creators who take tough shots and have necessary turnovers. WS useful: - Capturing cumulative season value tied to team success. - Comparing players’ total contribution on winning teams (with context). - Separating “played a lot and was efficient” from “played little.” WS misleading: - Defensive value attribution (DWS) for individuals. - Comparing players across very different team contexts (great team vs bad team). - Over-crediting players who are passengers on elite defenses/offenses due to minutes. Impact metrics useful: - Estimating independent effect on point differential with context controls. Misleading: - Small samples, unstable defensive estimates, lineup collinearity. So: use PER/WS as partial views, not as definitive rankings.
I want to push back: if PER is so flawed, why do some front offices still reference it (at least historically)? Is it just inertia?

Historically, a few reasons: 1) Availability and simplicity: - PER is easy to compute from box score and easy to communicate. - Before widespread play-by-play and tracking, it was one of the few “advanced” options. 2) It’s not random: - It correlates with good players and with scoring/creation, which are central. 3) It can be a screening tool: - For scouting a large pool (e.g., international leagues with limited data), a box-score index can flag players worth deeper evaluation. 4) Institutional memory: - People get used to a metric; it becomes part of the language. Modern front offices use more sophisticated internal models, but even then, simple metrics can be used as sanity checks or quick summaries. The key is not to treat PER as the final evaluation.
Speaking of other leagues, does PER translate to different leagues with different pace and style?

Not cleanly. PER assumes NBA-like relationships between box-score events and value: - The weights are tuned to NBA data/assumptions. In another league: - Pace, shot distribution, turnover rates, and substitution patterns differ. - The value of a steal or offensive rebound could differ due to transition frequency and scoring efficiency. You could compute PER mechanically, but interpretation changes. Ideally you’d: - Refit weights to that league’s outcomes (wins/point differential). - Normalize within that league. Otherwise you risk misranking players due to stylistic differences.
Let’s talk about metric gaming. Can players “optimize” for PER or WS in a way that doesn’t help the team?

In theory, yes—because any metric that rewards certain box-score events can be “gamed” at the margin. PER gaming tendencies: - Chase defensive rebounds (especially uncontested) to boost counting stats. - Hunt steals (gamble) to get high-weight events. - Avoid turnovers by making conservative plays, potentially reducing offensive creation. - Take only high-efficiency shots (avoid difficult but necessary attempts), which could hurt team offense if someone needs to take those shots. WS gaming is harder because it’s tied to team results and efficiency, but: - A player could avoid taking shots that would lower efficiency, boosting ORtg, even if the team needs shot creation. - Defensive shares are team-anchored; being on a strong defensive unit yields DWS regardless of individual intent. Real-world players don’t usually optimize explicitly for these metrics, but the point is: metric incentives don’t always align perfectly with winning, especially for box-score-only stats.
How do we evaluate “shot creation” beyond assists? Since box score only credits assists, not advantages.

Without tracking, proxies include: 1) Usage + efficiency: - High usage with good TS% indicates creation ability because self-created shots are harder. 2) Assist rate and potential assists (if available): - Traditional box score only has assists, but if you have play-by-play-derived “potential assists,” that’s better. 3) Turnover type proxies: - Not available in box score, but turnover rate relative to usage can indicate playmaking burden. 4) Team dependency: - Team offensive rating with the player on vs off can indicate creation beyond assists. 5) Free throw rate: - Drawing fouls is a creation skill; it’s in box score. Still, true “advantage creation” (forcing rotations, collapsing defense) is better captured by tracking (drives, paint touches) or by impact metrics.
If you had to pick only two publicly available metrics to use for player evaluation, what would they be and why?

If forced to pick two broad, public metrics: 1) BPM (and/or BPM split OBPM/DBPM) - Because it’s box-score-based but anchored to plus-minus outcomes and has a meaningful scale. 2) A hybrid impact metric (EPM/LEBRON/RAPTOR) or multi-year RAPM if available - Because it incorporates lineup results and captures non-box value like spacing and positional defense better than box-score-only. If those aren’t available, then: - BPM + WS (or WS/48) gives you a box-score impact estimate plus a wins/minutes value lens. PER would not be my top pick because BPM largely supersedes it for box-score summaries.
Let’s sanity check: does BPM have biases too? Give me a few known BPM “weird cases.”

Yes, BPM has biases: 1) It can love high-assist, low-turnover players even if their passing is conservative and doesn’t create high-value shots. Box score sees assists and low turnovers; it can’t see pass difficulty or advantage creation. 2) It can misread defensive value for players with steals/blocks: DBPM can overrate event defenders, similar to PER, though generally less extreme. 3) Position estimation issues: Players with unusual stat profiles can be misclassified, leading to odd weights. For example, a forward with very high assists might be treated like a guard, changing the baseline. 4) It can underrate pure shooters with gravity: If a player’s main value is spacing without huge box score volume, BPM might not fully capture it. 5) It can overrate rebound-heavy guards: Rebounds for guards can be valuable, but sometimes they’re scheme-driven (guards collecting long rebounds) and not necessarily indicative of impact. So BPM is better than PER in many cases, but still not definitive.
This is good. Now, I want you to walk through a hypothetical season line and estimate (qualitatively) what PER and WS would “think.” Give me a 3-and-D wing line and an interior big line.

Hypothetical 3-and-D wing (Wing X): - 32 mpg, 75 games - 12.5 PPG - 44% FG, 39% 3P on 6 attempts, 85% FT on low attempts - 2.2 AST, 1.0 TOV - 4.5 REB (mostly DRB) - 1.3 STL, 0.4 BLK - Strong defensive assignment PER view: - Efficient scoring helps, but low volume keeps scoring contribution moderate. - Low turnovers help. - Steals help. - Rebounds modest, blocks low. Likely PER around average to slightly above (say 13–16 depending on exact rates), not screaming “elite.” WS view: - OWS: decent if efficiency is above average and turnovers low, but limited possessions used means limited marginal offense. - DWS: depends on team defense. If team is good defensively and he plays big minutes, DWS could be solid. Total WS could be quite good because he plays a lot and is efficient, but might not match star totals. Hypothetical interior big (Big Y): - 30 mpg, 75 games - 16 PPG on 62% FG, 65% FT - 1.8 AST, 2.0 TOV - 11 REB (3 ORB, 8 DRB) - 0.8 STL, 2.0 BLK PER view: - Loves rebounds, especially ORB. - Loves blocks. - Loves high FG% finishing. Turnovers moderate but not huge. Likely high PER (20+), potentially star-ish even if his defense is mixed beyond blocks. WS view: - OWS: good if he’s efficient and uses many possessions without tanking ORtg. - DWS: if team defense is good, he’ll rack up DWS due to minutes and rebounds/blocks; if team defense is mediocre, DWS may be less. Total WS could be high because he’s productive and plays a lot. This illustrates the common bias: PER will more reliably elevate Big Y relative to Wing X, even if Wing X’s spacing/defense has comparable impact.
Now, can you explain why “assists” are an imperfect measure of playmaking, and how that affects PER and WS?

Assists are imperfect because: 1) They depend on teammate shot-making. A perfect pass that leads to a miss yields no assist. 2) They miss “hockey assists” and advantage passes. The pass that breaks the defense often isn’t the one that gets the assist. 3) They don’t encode pass difficulty or creation quality. A simple swing pass to an open shooter counts the same as a tight-window pass. 4) They’re system-dependent. Some offenses generate assists via ball movement; others rely on isolation where the creator generates shots without assists. Effect on PER: - PER credits assists, but if assists are system-inflated, PER may overrate that playmaking. - Conversely, creators in systems where teammates finish late or where the creator’s advantage leads to secondary actions might be underrated. Effect on WS: - Points produced via assists tries to estimate points created, but it’s still based on recorded assists. So the same limitations apply, though WS attempts to map assists to points more directly. Impact metrics can capture playmaking beyond assists if it consistently improves lineup offense.
I want to make sure we haven’t missed the “stat-head” basics. Define the common rate stats: AST%, TRB%, STL%, BLK%, TOV%, USG%, and how they feed into evaluation.

Definitions (conceptual): AST%: percentage of teammate field goals a player assisted while on the floor. - Measures playmaking volume relative to team makes. TRB%: percentage of available rebounds a player grabbed while on the floor. - Controls for pace and missed shots. STL%: percentage of opponent possessions that end with the player recording a steal while on the floor. BLK%: percentage of opponent 2-point field goal attempts blocked by the player while on the floor (definition can vary slightly). TOV%: turnovers per 100 plays/possessions used (often per 100 possessions used by the player). USG%: share of team possessions used by the player via FGA, FTA, and TOV. How they feed evaluation: - Combine USG% with TS% to evaluate scoring efficiency under load. - AST% with TOV% to evaluate playmaking efficiency. - TRB% for rebounding impact, especially for bigs. - STL%/BLK% as partial defensive indicators, but incomplete. These rate stats are also key inputs to models like BPM and underpin why PER/WS value certain players.
Great. Now, I want you to anticipate a rebuttal: “All these metrics are just proxies. Just watch the games.” How do you respond as an analytics person without being dismissive?

The best response is: watching and metrics answer different questions and can complement each other. - Film tells you “how” value is created: decision-making, positioning, scheme fit, what shots are being generated/allowed. - Metrics summarize “how much” impact shows up in outcomes and box score, and help compare across many players and games. Both have biases: - Film is subject to memory, narrative, and limited sample (you can’t watch everything equally carefully). - Metrics can miss context and misattribute value due to model limitations. A balanced approach: - Use metrics to flag patterns or discrepancies: “This player’s impact metrics are high despite modest box score—why?” - Use film to diagnose mechanisms. - Use multiple metrics to avoid overfitting to one lens. So it’s not “metrics vs watching”; it’s “use evidence from both.”
Before we wrap, I want a compact summary of the main flaws of PER and WS in bullet form, but still technical.

PER flaws: - Box-score-only; misses off-ball offense and most defense. - Heuristic weights; not fitted to modern outcome data. - Overweights event defense (STL/BLK) and rebounding, biasing toward bigs. - Penalizes high-usage difficult shot creation via missed shots/turnovers without crediting necessity. - Team pace adjustment uses team-level pace, not on-court context. - Normalized index; not directly interpretable in points/wins. WS flaws: - Defensive component anchored to team defensive rating; weak individual defensive identification. - DWS allocation relies on DRB/STL/BLK proxies and minutes; can misattribute. - Team wins constraint makes cross-team comparisons context-heavy. - Points-produced model depends on assists and box score; misses spacing/gravity and non-assist creation. - Nonlinearities and interactions in basketball aren’t fully captured by additive allocations.
That’s what I needed. One last thing: if someone insists on using PER, what are the “safe” ways to use it?

Safe-ish ways to use PER: - Use it as a within-role, within-season box-score productivity rate, not a universal ranking. - Pair it with minutes: high PER in tiny minutes is less meaningful. - Cross-check with TS%, usage, and turnover rate to see if the PER is driven by sustainable efficiency or by event spikes. - Don’t use it to evaluate defense-heavy players or compare wings to bigs without context. - Use it as a starting point, then confirm with BPM/impact metrics and on/off data. Used that way, PER can be a quick descriptive tool rather than a definitive value metric.
What are the greatest upsets in Olympic history where a massive underdog actually won?

A handful of Olympic upsets are so dramatic that they’ve become shorthand for “anything can happen,” even decades later. Here are some of the most memorable, with enough context to show why they were shocking at the time: 1) 1980 Ice Hockey (Men) — USA vs USSR (“Miracle on Ice”), Lake Placid - Why it was an upset: The Soviet team was essentially a world all-star roster, dominant in international play, and had just crushed the USA 10–3 in an exhibition. The USA roster was mostly college players. - What happened: The Americans took the Soviets to a frantic, high-tempo game and won 4–3. It wasn’t the gold-medal game, but it effectively swung the tournament; the USA later beat Finland to clinch gold. - Why it endures: It’s a rare combination of matchup disparity, political backdrop, and a game that was genuinely high quality. 2) 1936 Athletics — Jesse Owens undermines Nazi propaganda (not an upset in “underdog won” terms) - This is more “symbolic shock” than underdog victory; Owens was a top athlete. I mention it because people often confuse “memorable” with “upset.” If we stick strictly to underdogs, other track upsets fit better. 3) 2004 Marathon (Men) — Stefano Baldini (Italy) wins after favorites falter, Athens - Why it was an upset: Marathon favorites included several established winners; the race unfolded with tactical surges and attrition in brutal heat. - What happened: Baldini made a decisive move after others cracked; it’s a classic case of the “favorite’s race” becoming an “opportunist’s race.” 4) 2016 Gymnastics (Men’s rings) — Eleftherios Petrounias was favored, but other apparatus finals show bigger shockers - Gymnastics upsets tend to be “one wobble changes everything.” The most dramatic are when the top qualifier has a major error in finals and a lower qualifier hits cold. 5) 2012 Badminton scandal (not an upset; disqualifications) - Again, memorable but not an underdog win. True “upsets” exist in badminton, but the London scandal is more about format incentives. If you want a cleaner list of pure “massive underdog wins,” I’d focus on: - 1980 USA hockey over USSR - 1960 heavyweight boxing: Cassius Clay (later Ali) over Zbigniew Pietrzykowski (Clay was promising but not the established favorite in that final) - 2000 Sydney: Cathy Freeman was favored, so not an upset; but other track finals had surprise winners from outside the expected medal group - 2004 women’s gymnastics all-around: Carly Patterson over Khorkina/Hamm? (This is more “unexpected” than “massive underdog,” but still a surprise given the field and pressure) To tailor it: do you want “biggest upset” in terms of betting odds / pre-event ranking, or “most famous” in terms of cultural impact? And are team sports included, or only individual events?
Include team sports and individual events. I want the ones that were shocking in the moment, not just now in hindsight. Also, I’d love some older ones, not only modern.

Then I’d build a “shock-in-the-moment” list across eras, where the pre-event consensus strongly favored someone else and the final result immediately read as a stunner. Here’s a set that spans older to newer, mixing team and individual: A) Team-sport stunners 1) 1956 Water Polo (Men) — Hungary vs USSR (“Blood in the Water”) is iconic, but not an upset - Hungary was elite; the shock was political and violent, not an underdog win. I’ll keep it as a “memorable” note, but it’s not the underdog story you asked for. 2) 1972 Basketball (Men) — USSR defeats USA, Munich - Why it was shocking: The USA had never lost an Olympic men’s basketball game (a perfect Olympic record up to that point), even though the context matters (U.S. amateurs vs Soviet state-sponsored “amateurs”). - What happened: A chaotic final sequence with the clock reset controversially allowed the USSR to score a game-winning basket, 51–50. - Upset framing: It’s less “massive underdog” and more “the unthinkable happened”—the USA finally lost. 3) 1980 Ice Hockey (Men) — USA over USSR - The quintessential “massive underdog” team upset. 4) 2004 Football/Soccer (Men) — Argentina’s run isn’t an upset, but specific matches can be - Olympic men’s football has age restrictions now; “upsets” often happen due to U-23 dynamics.
